EDCS-00380923.text_edition: Cohors VI Thracum turma Ve Iuli
EDCS-00380923.translation: The Sixth Cohort of Thracians, in the fifth troop of Iulus.
EDCS-00380923.summary: This inscription is a military record indicating a unit and a specific troop commander or designator within the Sixth Thracian Cohort.

EDCS-00380924.text_edition: Aurelius Gaianus |(centurio) cohortis V Gallorum Antoninianae ex voto posuit
EDCS-00380924.translation: Aurelius Gaianus, centurion of the Fifth Cohort of Gauls, Antoniniana, placed this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-00380924.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Aurelius Gaianus, a centurion, to fulfill a vow, likely on behalf of his military unit.

EDCS-03300802.text_edition: Lucius Antonius Luci filius Quirina Valens Sagalasso
EDCS-03300802.translation: Lucius Antonius Valens, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, from Sagalassus.
EDCS-03300802.summary: This inscription is an identification of Lucius Antonius Valens, specifying his filiation, Roman tribe, and place of origin.

EDCS-03300808.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Iulia Charitine vixit annos XLV Titus Claudius Valerianus miles legionis VII Claudiae stipendiorum IIII vixit annos XXII Titus Claudius Valens veteranus ex tesserario legionis supra scriptae coniugi et filio karissimis bene merentibus posuit et se vivo et Iuliae Valentiae et Titis Claudiis Valentino Valenti et Valentioni filis fecit
EDCS-03300808.translation: To the Divine Shades. Julia Charitine lived 45 years. Titus Claudius Valerianus, a soldier of the Seventh Claudian Legion with four years of service, lived 22 years. Titus Claudius Valens, a veteran formerly a *tesserarius* (watchword keeper) of the aforementioned legion, set this up for his dearest wife and son, who deserved it well. And while he was alive, he also made it for Julia Valentia and his sons Titus Claudius Valentinus, Valens, and Valentio.
EDCS-03300808.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Titus Claudius Valens, a veteran *tesserarius*, for his deceased wife Julia Charitine and son Titus Claudius Valerianus, a soldier, and also prepared during his lifetime for his other living family members: Julia Valentia and sons Titus Claudius Valentinus, Valens, and Valentio.

EDCS-03700710.text_edition: Somno C CI votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-03700710.translation: To Sleep, C. C. I. willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-03700710.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to the god Somnus (Sleep), made by an individual identified by initials, in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-05000713.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Tattae Idati Ablabius et Pontia cum liberis
EDCS-05000713.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, to Tatta and Idatus, Ablabius and Pontia with their children.
EDCS-05000713.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, and possibly local deities Tatta and Idatus, by Ablabius and Pontia along with their children.

EDCS-05200610.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Melano Asclepiades ex voto paterno aram posuit
EDCS-05200610.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Melanus, Asclepiades placed this altar in fulfillment of his father's vow.
EDCS-05200610.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Melanus by Asclepiades, fulfilling a vow originally made by his father.

EDCS-06100501.text_edition: Herculi Augusto pro salute Marci Aureli Antonini Publius Flavius Felix Publi filius vicomagister consensu collegi vici sententia posuit ex vico Bubensium per Aelium Probum patrem
EDCS-06100501.translation: To Hercules Augustus, for the well-being of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, Publius Flavius Felix, son of Publius, a vicomagister, by the decree and with the consent of the college of the vicus, erected this from the Bubensian neighborhood, through Aelius Probus, a patron.
EDCS-06100501.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar or statue to Hercules Augustus for the well-being of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla) by Publius Flavius Felix, a vicomagister of the Bubensian Neighborhood, acting by decree and with the consent of the neighborhood's college, likely in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-06100502.text_edition: Dis Manibus Duriacus Ithotsi filius annorum L Valerius Victor veteranus legionis IIII Flaviae bene merito posuit
EDCS-06100502.translation: To the Divine Shades. Valerius Victor, a veteran of the Fourth Flavian Legion, erected this for the well-deserving Duriacus, son of Ithotsus, who was 50 years old.
EDCS-06100502.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a monument by Valerius Victor, a veteran of Legio IIII Flavia, in honor of Duriacus, son of Ithotsus, who died at the age of 50, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07000847.text_edition: Aelius Niceforus libertus ipse sibi et Bae coniugi
EDCS-07000847.translation: Aelius Niceforus, a freedman, for himself and for his wife Bae.
EDCS-07000847.summary: This inscription is a funerary or dedicatory monument set up by Aelius Niceforus, a freedman, for himself and his wife Bae.

EDCS-07600419.summary: This inscription identifies an individual named Felix, indicating their association with or belonging to someone named Romulus or a place named after him.

EDCS-08400457.summary: This inscription is a possessive statement, indicating that the object it adorns belonged to or was associated with Lucius Coelius Saturninus.

EDCS-08400458.summary: This inscription is a possessive statement, indicating that the object it adorns belonged to or was associated with Lucius Coelius Ingenuus.

EDCS-08400459.summary: This is a simple nominative or genitive dedication, likely part of a larger monument, recording the name of Lucius Coelius Primus.

EDCS-08400460.translation: Of Primitivus, decurion of the colony.
EDCS-08400460.summary: This fragment records the name and office of Primitivus, a decurion of an unnamed Roman colony, suggesting a civic or honorary monument.

EDCS-08400471.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Augusto Invicto milia passuum
EDCS-08400471.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Augustus the Invincible, [number of] miles.
EDCS-08400471.summary: This is a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Decius (reigned 249-251 AD), likely marking a distance along a Roman road.

EDCS-08500592.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Marcus Cocceius Viator vixit nnos LV Ulpia Annia vixit nnos XXXV hic siti sunt Cocceius Ingenuus parentibus pientissimis posuit
EDCS-08500592.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Cocceius Viator lived 55 years. Ulpia Annia lived 35 years. Here they lie. Cocceius Ingenuus placed this for his most devoted parents.
EDCS-08500592.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the deaths of Marcus Cocceius Viator and Ulpia Annia, who lived 55 and 35 years respectively, set up by their son, Cocceius Ingenuus, for his most devoted parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500596.text_edition: I a Ursula as servus vivis fecerunt
EDCS-08500596.translation: I.A. Ursula and Servus made this for the living.
EDCS-08500596.summary: This inscription records that Ursula and Servus (perhaps a slave or freedman) prepared a monument for themselves while still alive, a common practice for Roman funerary memorials.

EDCS-08600630.text_edition: Cohors I Lusitanorum centuria Claudi Laedius
EDCS-08600630.translation: First Cohort of Lusitanians, century of Claudius, Laedius.
EDCS-08600630.summary: This inscription identifies a military unit, the First Cohort of Lusitanians, and a specific century led by Claudius, mentioning the name Laedius, likely indicating a centurion or a soldier belonging to that century, perhaps from a building project or roster.

EDCS-08900588.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Caius Iulius Victorinus miles legionis VII Claudiae beneficiarius consularis votum solvit
EDCS-08900588.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, to Juno Regina, Gaius Julius Victorinus, a soldier of the Seventh Claudian Legion, a consular beneficiarius, paid his vow.
EDCS-08900588.summary: This inscription records Gaius Julius Victorinus, a soldier of the Seventh Claudian Legion and a consular beneficiarius, fulfilling a vow made to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina.

EDCS-08901136.text_edition: Impperatores Caessares Caius Valerius Aurelius Diocletianus et Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus PPii FFelices Invicti Auggusti et Flavius Valerius Constantius et Galerius Valerius Maximianus nobilissimi Caesares Germanici maximi Sarmatici maximi profuturum in aeternum rei publicae praesidium constituerunt
EDCS-08901136.translation: The Emperors Gaius Valerius Aurelius Diocletianus and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus, Pious, Fortunate, Undefeated Augusti, and Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximianus, most noble Caesars, supreme victors over the Germans and Sarmatians, established [this] as an eternal safeguard for the benefit of the Republic.
EDCS-08901136.summary: This inscription, likely from a building or milestone, commemorates the establishment of a public work by the four Tetrarchs (Emperors Diocletian and Maximian, and Caesars Constantius and Galerius) for the eternal benefit of the Roman state, dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-08901148.text_edition: Dis Manibus Sexti Valeri Sexti liberti Palatina Zotici vixit annos XLII hic situs est Sextus Valerius Hilarus Augustalis coloniae Ratiariae heres faciendum curavit
EDCS-08901148.translation: To the Divine Manes. Sextus Valerius Zoticus, freedman of Sextus, of the Palatina tribe, lived 42 years. He is laid here. Sextus Valerius Hilarus, an Augustalis of the colony Ratiaria, his heir, took care to have this monument made.
EDCS-08901148.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Valerius Zoticus, a freedman, erected by his heir, Sextus Valerius Hilarus, an Augustalis of the colony Ratiaria, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08901149.text_edition: Divos testor  Dis Manibus Antoniae Antoni libertae Nicopoli vixit nnos XXX hic sita est Marcus Antonius Glycon coniugi et libertae bene merenti posuit
EDCS-08901149.translation: I call the gods to witness. To the Divine Manes of Antonia Nicopolis, freedwoman of Antonius. She lived 30 years; she lies here. Marcus Antonius Glycon erected this for his well-deserving wife and freedwoman.
EDCS-08901149.summary: This funerary inscription records a tombstone erected by Marcus Antonius Glycon for his freedwoman and wife, Antonia Nicopolis, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-08901150.text_edition: Lucius Septimius Dulacenus ex signifero vixit annis XL militavit annis XXII Antoninus Aiacartus hic situs sunt
EDCS-08901150.translation: Lucius Septimius Dulacenus, formerly a standard-bearer, lived for 40 years and served for 22 years. Antoninus Aiacartus. They are buried here.
EDCS-08901150.summary: This funerary inscription records the lives of two individuals, Lucius Septimius Dulacenus, a former standard-bearer who lived for 40 years and served for 22 years, and Antoninus Aiacartus, stating that they are both interred at this location.

EDCS-08901151.text_edition: Dis Manibus Caio Aemilio Cai liberto Sabiniano vixit annos LV hic situs est BA
EDCS-08901151.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Gaius Aemilius Sabinianus, freedman of Gaius, who lived 55 years; he is buried here. BA.
EDCS-08901151.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes for Gaius Aemilius Sabinianus, a freedman, recording his age and place of burial.

EDCS-09100862.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio Illyrici pro salute Bellici Augusti nostri vilici stationis Petobionensis sic et Moesiae regionis Aquensis Castricius Bellici vikarius sic votum solvit libens merito anni XXV Kalendis Februariis Fusco II Dextro consulibus
EDCS-09100862.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Genius of Illyricum, for the well-being of our Bellicus Augustus, manager of the Petobio station and likewise of the Aquensian region of Moesia. Castricius, deputy of Bellicus, thus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow on the Kalends of February, in the twenty-fifth year, in the consulship of Fuscus (for the second time) and Dextrus.
EDCS-09100862.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Genius of Illyricum, made by Castricius, the deputy of the imperial manager Bellicus Augustus, in fulfillment of a vow for Bellicus's welfare, dated to February 1st, 227 AD.

EDCS-09100863.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Melano Cidiesso Aelius Octavianus veteranus cum suis votum posuit
EDCS-09100863.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Melanus Cidiessus, Aelius Octavianus, a veteran, with his family, set up this vow.
EDCS-09100863.summary: This inscription records a votive offering dedicated to Jupiter Best and Greatest, invoked as Melanus Cidiessus, by Aelius Octavianus, a veteran, together with his household.

EDCS-09100864.text_edition: Dis Manibus Valens sitae sibi coniugi Daedae Iunctae idemque Aniae Dasi vivae vivus posuit
EDCS-09100864.translation: To the Divine Manes. Valens, while still living, set this up for his deceased wife, Daeda Iuncta, and also for Ania Dasis, who is still alive.
EDCS-09100864.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Valens for his deceased wife, Daeda Iuncta, and also for Ania Dasis, who is still living, presumably intended for a shared burial.

EDCS-09100865.text_edition: Dis Manibus Possidonius Biti vixit annos LXX Iusta coniunx et heres marito et sibi viva bene merentibus posuit
EDCS-09100865.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Possidonius Biti lived 70 years. Iusta, his wife and heir, while alive, placed this monument for her well-deserving husband and for herself.
EDCS-09100865.summary: This funerary inscription, a tombstone, was dedicated by Iusta, the wife and heir, to her husband Possidonius Biti, recording his age of 70, and also for herself while she was still living, expressing gratitude to both as 'well-deserving'.

EDCS-09100866.text_edition: Dis Manibus Andius Austeri filius vixit annos LXX
EDCS-09100866.translation: To the Divine Manes. Andius, son of Austerus, lived 70 years.
EDCS-09100866.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes for Andius, son of Austerus, recording his age at death.

EDCS-09100867.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aelius Caeanus miles legionis VII vexit sic annos XXV Desumena coniux
EDCS-09100867.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aelius Caeanus, a soldier of the 7th legion, lived for 25 years. Desumena, his wife [set this up].
EDCS-09100867.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by Desumena, the wife, to her husband Aelius Caeanus, a soldier of the 7th legion, who died at the age of 25.

EDCS-09100868.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelia Petronia vixit annis XII Aurelius Papilus et Aurelia Caia vivi sibi et filiae bene merenti posuerunt hic sita est
EDCS-09100868.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelia Petronia lived for 12 years. Aurelius Papilus and Aurelia Caia, while living, erected this for themselves and for their well-deserving daughter. Here she lies.
EDCS-09100868.summary: This funerary inscription records that Aurelius Papilus and Aurelia Caia, while still alive, erected a monument for themselves and for their daughter, Aurelia Petronia, who died at the age of 12.

EDCS-09100869.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aelia Clementilla vixit annos XVII summae intergritatis feminae Aurelius Maior ex protectoribus divini lateris coniugi obsequentissimae posuit
EDCS-09100869.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aelia Clementilla lived 17 years, a woman of the highest integrity. Aurelius Maior, one of the imperial bodyguards, erected this for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-09100869.summary: This is a funerary inscription (likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century AD) placed by Aurelius Maior, a former imperial bodyguard, in memory of his very devoted wife, Aelia Clementilla, who died at the young age of 17.

EDCS-09100870.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelius Caianuns salgam=MAGarius#salgamarius#SALMAGARIUS vixit annis Aurelia Alexandria vixit annis Z hic siti sunt collegius sic DEIRB ex testamento AlEexandriae#Alexandriae#AEEXANDRIAE supra scriptae quot sic suis sumptibus fecerat per senatus consultum posuerunt sic
EDCS-09100870.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelius Caianuns, a salt merchant, lived for Z years. Aurelia Alexandria lived for Z years. They are laid here. The college, thus DEIRB, placed this monument by a decree of the Senate, according to the will of Alexandria mentioned above, who had had it made at her own expense.
EDCS-09100870.summary: This inscription records a tombstone for Aurelius Caianuns and Aurelia Alexandria, erected by the college 'DEIRB' according to a senatorial decree and Alexandria's will, as she had funded its creation.

EDCS-09100871.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelius Stilo Babonis vixit annos L hic situs est Aurelia Ziza Merulae marito et Aurelius Aquilinus patri piissimo et sibi vivi faciendum curaverunt
EDCS-09100871.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelius Stilo, (son) of Babo, lived 50 years; he lies here. Aurelia Ziza, (daughter) of Merula, for her husband, and Aurelius Aquilinus for his most devoted father, and for themselves, had this monument made while they were still alive.
EDCS-09100871.summary: This funerary monument records the burial of Aurelius Stilo Babonis, who lived 50 years, and was erected by his wife Aurelia Ziza (daughter of Merula) and his son Aurelius Aquilinus both for him and for themselves while still living, typical of the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-09100872.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelia Iusta se viva posuit Ulpio Castino aedelicio sic vixit annis VLV filii Castus vivit sic annis XIIII Iovius annis XI Aurelianus annis X Ninis annis III Utinadius annis IIII Ulpia Castina coniugi bene merito opto sit terra levis
EDCS-09100872.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelia Iusta, while still alive, set this up for Ulpius Castinus, an aedile, who lived for 55 years. Their sons: Castus, who lives, is 14 years old; Iovius is 11 years old; Aurelianus is 10 years old; Ninis is 3 years old; Utinadius is 4 years old. Ulpia Castina [dedicates this] to her well-deserving husband. I pray that the earth may be light to him.
EDCS-09100872.summary: This funerary inscription, likely a tombstone from the Imperial Roman period, was set up by Aurelia Iusta (also known as Ulpia Castina) while she was alive, for her husband, Ulpius Castinus, an aedile, and commemorates their children, some of whom were still living.

EDCS-09100873.text_edition: Caius Domitius Lucinianus   filius beneficiarius consularis   foro posuit
EDCS-09100873.translation: Gaius Domitius Lucinianus, son, beneficiary of the consular governor, placed this in the forum.
EDCS-09100873.summary: This inscription records that Gaius Domitius Lucinianus, identified as the son of another individual and a beneficiary (assistant) to a consular governor, erected a monument in the forum.

EDCS-09100874.text_edition: Aurelius Caius centurio cohortis I Cretum  Aurelius Caius centurio cohortis I Cretum
EDCS-09100874.translation: Aurelius Caius, centurion of the First Cohort of Cretans.
EDCS-09100874.summary: This inscription records the name and military rank of Aurelius Caius, a centurion of the First Cohort of Cretans, likely identifying him on a personal monument such as a tombstone or statue base.

EDCS-09300644.text_edition: I colonia Singidunensium Quinto Ulpio DAP legato Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-09300644.translation: First colony of Singidunum. To Quintus Ulpius D.A.P., legate of Augustus pro praetore.
EDCS-09300644.summary: This is an honorary inscription from the First Colony of Singidunum dedicated to Quintus Ulpius, a legate of Augustus, likely commemorating his service or benefactions to the colony.

EDCS-09401519.text_edition: hic sita est vixit annos XXVIII menses VI vita integerrima Iulius Mucianus actuarius coniugi
EDCS-09401519.translation: Here she lies. She lived 28 years, 6 months, a life of utmost integrity. Julius Mucianus, the scribe, [erected this] for his wife.
EDCS-09401519.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of a woman who lived for 28 years and 6 months, dedicated by her husband, Julius Mucianus, who held the position of an actuarius (scribe or accountant).

EDCS-09401520.text_edition: Flavia Secundina hic sita est vixit annos |(circiter XXX Phoebio coniugi
EDCS-09401520.translation: Flavia Secundina is buried here. She lived approximately 30 years. To her husband Phoebius.
EDCS-09401520.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Secundina, stating her age at death and that it was made by her husband Phoebius.

EDCS-09401521.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Flavius Iustinianus, indicating he lived for an unspecified number of years, with some additional abbreviated text.

EDCS-09401557.summary: This inscription records the fabrication by Valerius Messor, likely on a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-09700324.text_edition: Aniensis Foro Iulio miles legionis I Italicae hic situs est nnum XVIII Lucius Octavius Pulcher |(centurio) legionis I Italicae cognato suo fecit
EDCS-09700324.translation: Aniensis, from Forum Iulium, a soldier of the First Italian Legion, is buried here. He lived for 18 years. Lucius Octavius Pulcher, a centurion of the First Italian Legion, made this for his relative.
EDCS-09700324.summary: This is a funerary inscription (tombstone) erected by Lucius Octavius Pulcher, a centurion of the First Italian Legion, for his 18-year-old relative, Aniensis, a soldier of the same legion, who was from Forum Iulium.

EDCS-09701192.text_edition: Caius Antonius Eutyches Antoniae Claudiae patronae vivae posuit
EDCS-09701192.translation: Gaius Antonius Eutyches placed this for his patroness, Antonia Claudia, while she was alive.
EDCS-09701192.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication placed by Gaius Antonius Eutyches for his living patroness, Antonia Claudia, indicating a praiseworthy act or the preparation of a tomb during her lifetime.

EDCS-10000137.translation: The century of Verus, Gaius Valerius Crescens.
EDCS-10000137.summary: This is a military inscription identifying Gaius Valerius Crescens as belonging to the century of Verus.

EDCS-10000139.summary: This is a military inscription marking the century of a person named Axsius, likely on a building block or a boundary stone.

EDCS-10000140.text_edition: Dis Manibus Domitia Panthia vixit nnos XVIIII menses IIII dies XX quae sine data morte mortua est pater pientissimus se vivo memoriam posuit filiae pientissimae fec(it)
EDCS-10000140.translation: To the Divine Shades. Domitia Panthia lived 19 years, 4 months, 20 days, who died an untimely death. Her most devoted father, while still alive, erected this memorial for his most devoted daughter.
EDCS-10000140.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the premature death of Domitia Panthia at the age of 19, dedicated by her devoted father during his lifetime as a memorial.

EDCS-10000141.text_edition: Deam Hecaten sic Triviae Triformi Valerius Crescentio miles legionis IIII Flaviae Maximinianae pro se et suis votum posuit libens merito
EDCS-10000141.translation: To the goddess Hecate, also known as triple-formed Trivia, Valerius Crescentio, a soldier of the Fourth Flavian Maximinian Legion, willingly and deservedly placed this vow for himself and his household.
EDCS-10000141.summary: This inscription records a religious dedication by Valerius Crescentio, a soldier of the Fourth Flavian Maximinian Legion, to the goddess Hecate (identified as triple-formed Trivia), made on behalf of himself and his family in fulfillment of a vow during the late Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000144.text_edition: Dis Manibus Attia Soteris vixit annos V hic sita est
EDCS-10000144.translation: To the Divine Manes. Attia Soteris lived for 5 years; she is interred here.
EDCS-10000144.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Attia Soteris, who died at the age of five and is buried at this location.

EDCS-10000152.text_edition: Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Saturninus vixit annos V menses III Flavia Cornelia mater faciendum curavit
EDCS-10000152.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Titus Flavius Saturninus lived for 5 years and 3 months. His mother, Flavia Cornelia, took care of having this made.
EDCS-10000152.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely from the Imperial period, dedicated by Flavia Cornelia for her young son, Titus Flavius Saturninus, who died at the age of five years and three months.

EDCS-10000154.text_edition: Dis Manibus Gaius Acilius Crispus vixit annis XXXV hic situs est Gaius Acilius Dexter filio pientissimo faciendum curavit
EDCS-10000154.translation: To the Divine Shades. Gaius Acilius Crispus lived for 35 years; here he is laid. Gaius Acilius Dexter had this made for his most dutiful son.
EDCS-10000154.summary: This funerary inscription records a tombstone set up by Gaius Acilius Dexter for his deceased son, Gaius Acilius Crispus, who lived for 35 years.

EDCS-10000175.text_edition: Tiberio Claudio Drusi filio Caesare Augusto Germanico pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate VI consule designato IIII legio IIII Scythica legio V Macedonica montibus excisis factisque anconibus sub cura Marti Macri legati Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-10000175.translation: To Tiberius Claudius, son of Drusus, Caesar Augustus Germanicus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the sixth time, and designated consul for the fourth time, the Fourth Scythian Legion and the Fifth Macedonian Legion cut through mountains and constructed buttresses, under the supervision of Martius Macer, imperial legate with propraetorian power.
EDCS-10000175.summary: This inscription records an engineering feat—the cutting through of mountains and construction of buttresses by the Fourth Scythian Legion and the Fifth Macedonian Legion, under the supervision of governor Martius Macer, dedicated to Emperor Claudius around 46-47 CE.

EDCS-10000179.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo ceterisque dis deabus huiusce loci pro salute domini nostri M Ucenteus beneficiarius consularis legionis VII Claudiae votum solvit
EDCS-10000179.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the other gods and goddesses of this place, for the well-being of our master, M. Ucenteus, a consular *beneficiarius* of the Seventh Claudian Legion, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10000179.summary: This inscription records a religious dedication by M. Ucenteus, a *beneficiarius consularis* of the Legio VII Claudia, who fulfilled a vow made to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and other local deities on behalf of 'our master' (likely the emperor).

EDCS-10000181.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo et Nemesi Reginae Genio stationis M Dardaniae Septimius Vitalis beneficiarius consularis legionis VII Claudiae votum libens solvit
EDCS-10000181.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to Nemesis the Queen, and to the Genius of the station in Moesia Dardania, Septimius Vitalis, a consular *beneficiarius* of the Seventh Claudian Legion, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10000181.summary: This inscription records a religious dedication made by Septimius Vitalis, a *beneficiarius consularis* of Legio VII Claudia, to Jupiter, Nemesis, and the Genius of a station in Moesia Dardania, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-10000484.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelio Tarae veterano cohortis III Campestris vexillario vixit annos LX Ulpia Marcia coniugi bene merenti posuit
EDCS-10000484.translation: To the Divine Shades. Ulpia Marcia placed this monument for her well-deserving husband, Aurelius Taras, a veteran and standard-bearer of the Third Campestrian Cohort, who lived 60 years.
EDCS-10000484.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Ulpia Marcia to her husband, Aurelius Taras, a veteran and standard-bearer of the Third Campestrian Cohort, who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-10000485.text_edition: Dis Manibus Caerelia Quintilla vixit aannos XL
EDCS-10000485.translation: To the Divine Manes. Caerelia Quintilla lived for 40 years.
EDCS-10000485.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caerelia Quintilla, recording her age at death as 40 years.

EDCS-10000486.text_edition: Crispus D domo Inaquiese vixsit sic annis XXX meruit annis VI miles cohortis I Cantabrorum heres faciendum curavit
EDCS-10000486.translation: Crispus D, from Inaquis, lived for 30 years; he served for 6 years as a soldier of the First Cohort of Cantabrians. His heir saw to its erection.
EDCS-10000486.summary: This funerary inscription for Crispus D, a soldier of the First Cohort of Cantabrians, records his age and years of military service, and was erected by his heir.

EDCS-10000487.text_edition: Marcius Publius Valerius Clemens Marci filius vixit nnos V hic situs est memoriam fecit
EDCS-10000487.translation: Marcius Publius Valerius Clemens, son of Marcus, lived for 5 years. He lies here. This memorial was made.
EDCS-10000487.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Marcius Publius Valerius Clemens, a young boy who lived five years, with the monument erected by his family.

EDCS-10000488.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Pompeius Isauricus ex votu sic posuit
EDCS-10000488.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Pompeius Isauricus thus placed this as a result of a vow.
EDCS-10000488.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication made by Pompeius Isauricus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-10000490.text_edition: Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate V pater patriae consul IIII ob periculum cataractarum derivato flumine tutam Danuvi navigationem fecit
EDCS-10000490.translation: The Emperor Caesar Trajan Augustus Germanicus, son of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the fifth time, Father of the Fatherland, and Consul for the fourth time, made the navigation of the Danube safe by diverting the river due to the danger of the cataracts.
EDCS-10000490.summary: This imperial inscription, dating to 101-102 CE, records Emperor Trajan's engineering project to improve Danube navigation by diverting the river around dangerous cataracts.

EDCS-10000491.text_edition: Genio sancto paterno Daciarum Antonius Maximus |(centurio) legionis princeps praetorii voto libenter soluto
EDCS-10000491.translation: To the holy paternal Genius of the Dacias, Antonius Maximus, centurion of the legion, chief of the praetorium, gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10000491.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication by Antonius Maximus, a centurion of a legion and chief of the praetorium, to the holy paternal Genius of the Dacias, likely dating from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-10000494.text_edition: Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Crescens vixit annis XXXV PTE
EDCS-10000494.translation: To the Divine Manes. Titus Flavius Crescens lived for 35 years. Placed with pious devotion.
EDCS-10000494.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Crescens, indicating he lived for 35 years and was commemorated with pious devotion.

EDCS-10000496.text_edition: Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Crispo veterano patri et Sallustiae Epictuni matri et Ulpiis Caio et Herculiano fratribus hic siti sunt Marcus Ulpius Serulenus bene merentibus posuit
EDCS-10000496.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Ulpius Serulenus placed this for his well-deserving father, Marcus Ulpius Crispus, a veteran; and for his mother, Sallustia Epictune; and for his brothers, Ulpius Gaius and Ulpius Herculianus. They are buried here.
EDCS-10000496.summary: This funerary inscription, likely a tombstone, records the dedication by Marcus Ulpius Serulenus to his deceased father (a veteran), mother, and two brothers, who are interred at this location.

EDCS-10000503.text_edition: Deo Soli Invicto Mithrae Claudius Diogenes hastatus legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10000503.translation: To the Unconquered Sun God Mithras, Claudius Diogenes, a hastatus of the Seventh Claudian Legion, pious and faithful, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10000503.summary: This votive inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the Unconquered Sun God Mithras by Claudius Diogenes, a *hastatus* of the Seventh Claudian Legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000504.text_edition: Deo Soli Invicto Mithrae Valerius Liberalis  legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10000504.translation: To the god Sol Invictus Mithras, Valerius Liberalis of the Seventh Legion Claudia Pia Fidelis willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10000504.summary: This is a Roman votive dedication by Valerius Liberalis, a soldier of the Seventh Legion Claudia Pia Fidelis, to the god Sol Invictus Mithras, recording the fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-10000505.text_edition: Dis Manibus Tito Aurelio Mercatori veterano ex imaginifero legionis VII Claudiae vixit nnos L et Aurelio Marciano militi legionis eiusdem vixit nnos XXX militavit nnos V Titus Aurelius Quintus veteranus legionis eiusdem hic positi sunt
EDCS-10000505.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Titus Aurelius Mercator, veteran, formerly *imaginifer* of the Seventh Claudia Legion, who lived 50 years; and for Aurelius Marcianus, a soldier of the same legion, who lived 30 years and served 5 years. They were placed here by Titus Aurelius Quintus, veteran of the same legion.
EDCS-10000505.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication by Titus Aurelius Quintus, a veteran of the Seventh Claudia Legion, of a monument for Titus Aurelius Mercator, an *imaginifer* veteran, and Aurelius Marcianus, a soldier, both also of the Seventh Claudia Legion.

EDCS-10000506.text_edition: Dis Manibus Calliope ancillae Luci P Gaiani |(centurionis) legionis IIII Flaviae Lucius P Huacinthus sic coniugi dulcissimae monumentum posuit vixit annos XVIII
EDCS-10000506.translation: To the Divine Shades. Lucius P. Hyacinthus dedicated this monument to his sweetest wife, Calliope, a slave-woman of Lucius P. Gaianus, a centurion of the Fourth Flavian Legion. She lived for 18 years.
EDCS-10000506.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Lucius P. Hyacinthus for his wife Calliope, an 18-year-old slave of a centurion from the Fourth Flavian Legion.

EDCS-10000507.text_edition: IDAVNI Apollini Q S Dianae Silvano Aelius Valentinus IIvir coloniae votum libens posuit
EDCS-10000507.translation: Aelius Valentinus, duovir of the colony, willingly fulfilled his vow to Idavnus Apollo, and to Diana and Silvanus.
EDCS-10000507.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Aelius Valentinus, a duovir of a Roman colony, who willingly fulfilled a vow to the deities Idavnus Apollo, Diana, and Silvanus.

EDCS-10000508.text_edition: CC AA  Alexander et M-- quinquennalis collegii I
EDCS-10000508.translation: C.C.A.A. Alexander and M[---], quinquennial officer of collegium I.
EDCS-10000508.summary: This inscription records Alexander and M[---] as quinquennial officers of a collegium, likely a professional or religious association.

EDCS-10000509.summary: This inscription records a donation made by T. Epagathus, likely to a deity or a public institution.

EDCS-10000510.translation: to Rio Maximus, father. Maximus, son.
EDCS-10000510.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely for Rio Maximus, erected by his son Maximus.

EDCS-10000517.text_edition: Invicto et super omnes indulgentissimo Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano nobilissimo principi dedicante Titieno Vero viro egregio procuratori mmetallorum DDardanorum ordo colonorum devotus numini maiestatique eius
EDCS-10000517.translation: To the unconquered and, above all, most benevolent Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, most noble prince; dedicated by Titienus Verus, distinguished man, procurator of the Dardanian mines, on behalf of the order of colonists, devoted to his divinity and majesty.
EDCS-10000517.summary: This inscription records a dedication by an order of colonists, expressing their devotion to the divinity and majesty of Emperor Gordian III, overseen by Titienus Verus, the procurator of the Dardanian mines.

EDCS-10000518.text_edition: IN PI tribunicia potestate  imperatori  consuli III patri patriae  Amandus Augusti libertus procurator patrono indulgentissimo sua pecunia posuit
EDCS-10000518.translation: To the Pious one, holding tribunician power, to the emperor, consul for the third time, father of the country: Amandus, an imperial freedman and procurator, erected this at his own expense for his most indulgent patron.
EDCS-10000518.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Amandus, an imperial freedman and procurator, to an unnamed emperor, his 'most indulgent patron,' identified by titles including 'Pious,' 'holder of tribunician power,' 'consul for the third time,' and 'father of the country,' funded entirely by Amandus himself, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-10000519.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus maximus Britnnicus maximus Germanicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVIIII imperator III consul IIII pater patriae proconsul  temporibus suis TIVS TAT
EDCS-10000519.translation: The Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, greatest victor over the Parthians, greatest victor over the Britons, greatest victor over the Germans, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 19th time, acclaimed imperator three times, consul four times, father of his country, proconsul, during his own times, Tius Tat.
EDCS-10000519.summary: This inscription records the extensive imperial titulature of Emperor Caracalla, dating it to approximately 215-216 AD, and likely formed part of a public monument or dedication, possibly mentioning a dedicant or a specific action at its fragmentary end.

EDCS-10000520.summary: This fragment likely comes from an official dedication or public monument mentioning the Emperor Antoninus Augustus, probably during his consulate.

EDCS-10000523.text_edition: Dis Manibus Titi Aeli Gridis vixit nnos XXXV Sempronii
EDCS-10000523.translation: To the Divine Manes of Titus Aelius Gridis, who lived 35 years. Of Sempronius [---].
EDCS-10000523.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Aelius Gridis, who lived 35 years, possibly set up by or related to Sempronius.

EDCS-10000524.text_edition: Dis Manibus Catti Lam vixit annos RAM
EDCS-10000524.translation: To the Divine Manes of Cattus Lam, who lived for many years.
EDCS-10000524.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Cattus Lam, indicating he lived for an unspecified but significant number of years.

EDCS-10000526.translation: To the Divine Manes of Riscas Abudias.
EDCS-10000526.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Riscas Abudias.

EDCS-10000527.text_edition: Aurelio  liberto vixit annos defuncto  DI AV VVA VI  VGII HANO  posuit
EDCS-10000527.translation: To Aurelius, freedman, who lived years. Deceased. [---] placed this.
EDCS-10000527.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius, a freedman, noting his death and erected by an unnamed individual or group.

EDCS-10000529.text_edition: Dis Manibus  lis Auggustorum  veBrna#verna#VBRNA L vixit annos XXXV mana coniugi bene merenti posuit
EDCS-10000529.translation: To the Divine Spirits. To Aelia Verna, freedwoman of the Augusti, who lived 35 years. Mana erected this for her well-deserving spouse.
EDCS-10000529.summary: This funerary inscription records Mana's dedication to her spouse, Aelia Verna, an imperial freedwoman who lived 35 years, dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-10000540.text_edition: Publius Licinius Publi filius Papiria Aelianus decurio municipiorum Ulpiani et Aeli Viminacii et Aeliani et Ulpia Cassa eius porticum incendio consumptam sua pecunia restituerunt
EDCS-10000540.translation: Publius Licinius Aelianus, son of Publius, of the Papirian tribe, a decurion of the municipalities of Ulpianum, Aelium Viminacium, and Aelianum, and his wife Ulpia Cassa, restored at their own expense a portico that had been consumed by fire.
EDCS-10000540.summary: This inscription records that Publius Licinius Aelianus, a decurion of several municipalities, and his wife Ulpia Cassa, funded the restoration of a public portico that had been destroyed by fire.

EDCS-10000545.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Melano Martinus Septimius sonius icanus ex voto posuerunt
EDCS-10000545.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Melanus, Martinus, Septimius, and Icanus placed this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-10000545.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, dedicated by Melanus, Martinus, Septimius, and Icanus in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-10000546.text_edition: Fortunae Dominae Aurelius Cas decurio posuit
EDCS-10000546.translation: To Fortuna Domina. Aurelius Cassius, decurion, placed this.
EDCS-10000546.summary: This is a votive inscription to Fortuna Domina, dedicated by Aurelius Cassius, a decurion.

EDCS-10000547.translation: Sacred to the God Silvanus. Rufus [placed this].
EDCS-10000547.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god Silvanus by Rufus.

EDCS-10000548.text_edition: Dis Manibus Septimius Dassius veteranus cohortis primae Montanorum et Aelia Nanea coniux eius et Aeliae Celsinae quae vixit annis XXX et Aeliae Romanae quae vixit annis XXV filiabus pientissimis et sibi vivi faciendum curarunt et Annio Timenti adfini nostro
EDCS-10000548.translation: To the Divine Shades. Septimius Dassius, a veteran of the First Cohort of Montani, and Aelia Nanea, his wife, had this monument made while they were alive for themselves, and for their most devoted daughters, Aelia Celsina, who lived for 30 years, and Aelia Romana, who lived for 25 years, and for Annius Timentius, our kinsman.
EDCS-10000548.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument prepared by Septimius Dassius, a veteran, and his wife Aelia Nanea, for themselves while living, for their two deceased daughters Aelia Celsina and Aelia Romana, and for their kinsman Annius Timentius, reflecting typical Roman Imperial family dedications.

EDCS-10000550.text_edition: Nemesi Augustae sacrum Publius Petilius Mercator decurio  coloniae IIvir
EDCS-10000550.translation: Sacred to Nemesis Augusta. Publius Petilius Mercator, decurion of the colony, duumvir [placed this].
EDCS-10000550.summary: This is a votive inscription to Nemesis Augusta, dedicated by Publius Petilius Mercator, a decurion of the colony and a duumvir.

EDCS-10000553.text_edition: Manius Epidius Mani filius Vicetia Pudens Menenia vixit nnos LX militavit nnos XXX Charitio Voconia coniux coniugi hic situs est
EDCS-10000553.translation: Manius Epidius Pudens, son of Manius, from Vicetia, of the Menenian tribe, lived 60 years and served 30 years in the military. Charitio Voconia, his wife, [erected this] for her husband. He is buried here.
EDCS-10000553.summary: This funerary inscription, erected by Charitio Voconia for her husband Manius Epidius Pudens, records his name, parentage, origin, tribal affiliation, age at death (60), and military service duration (30 years).

EDCS-10000555.text_edition: Dis Manibus Atilius Eucharistus Atiliae Veneriae uxori suae bene merenti posuit hic sita est
EDCS-10000555.translation: To the Divine Shades. Atilius Eucharistus set this up for his well-deserving wife, Atilia Veneria. She lies here.
EDCS-10000555.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a tombstone, where Atilius Eucharistus dedicated it to his deceased, well-deserving wife, Atilia Veneria.

EDCS-10000558.text_edition: Iulia Severae liberta Tyche vixit annis L hic sita est
EDCS-10000558.translation: Tyche, a freedwoman of Iulia Severa, lived for 50 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-10000558.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Tyche, a freedwoman, indicating her lifespan and burial place, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10000559.text_edition: Caius Valerius Valens vixit annos XVII et Caius Valerius Maximus vixit annos XVIII hic siti sunt Caius Valerius Lucius pater et Caelia Veneria mater filis at sic spem vitae studiis perductis in qua fortuna et fato deceptis faciendum curaverunt
EDCS-10000559.translation: Caius Valerius Valens lived 17 years, and Caius Valerius Maximus lived 18 years; here they are buried. Caius Valerius Lucius, father, and Caelia Veneria, mother, had this made for their sons, whose hope for life, cultivated through studies, was thus betrayed by fortune and fate.
EDCS-10000559.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Caius Valerius Lucius and Caelia Veneria for their two young sons, Caius Valerius Valens and Caius Valerius Maximus, lamenting their untimely deaths despite their dedication to studies.

EDCS-10000561.text_edition: Mesta Didae Sitae filia vixit nns XX hic sita est Lucius Dicconis filius coniunx et Dida Sitae pater et Mescena mater et Sita Mesti avius et Mania avia piissimae faciendum curaverunt
EDCS-10000561.translation: Mesta, daughter of Dida Sita, lived 20 years. Here she is laid. Lucius, son of Dicco, her husband, and Dida Sita, her father, and Mescena, her mother, and Sita Mesti, her grandfather, and Mania, her grandmother, most dutifully arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-10000561.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Mesta, who died at age 20, erected by her husband Lucius, her parents Dida Sita and Mescena, and her grandparents Sita Mesti and Mania.

EDCS-10000568.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae Claudius Alexander et Petilia Bassilla ex voto posuerunt
EDCS-10000568.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to Juno the Queen, Claudius Alexander and Petilia Bassilla set this up in accordance with a vow.
EDCS-10000568.summary: This inscription records a religious dedication made by Claudius Alexander and Petilia Bassilla to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, likely an altar or a monument erected in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-10000572.text_edition: Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Rufus vixit nnos LXV hic situs est Claudia Licinia coniux et Iulii Rufus et Rufinus patri bene merenti fecerunt
EDCS-10000572.translation: To the Divine Shades. Caius Julius Rufus lived 65 years and lies here. His wife, Claudia Licinia, and his sons, Julius Rufus and Rufinus, made this for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-10000572.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Caius Julius Rufus, who lived 65 years, by his wife Claudia Licinia and his two sons, Julius Rufus and Rufinus, presumably during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10000577.text_edition: Dis Manibus Sabino Antio |(centurioni) cohortis I Euporus et Fortunata patrono bene merenti posuerunt vixit annos XXXV
EDCS-10000577.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Euporus and Fortunata erected this monument to Sabinus Antiocus, centurion of the First Cohort, their well-deserving patron. He lived for 35 years.
EDCS-10000577.summary: This Roman funerary inscription was dedicated by Euporus and Fortunata to their well-deserving patron, Sabinus Antiocus, a centurion of the First Cohort, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-10000578.text_edition: Dis Manibus Ulpius Clemens vixit nnos II hic situs est sit tibi terra levis fui quod tu es eris quod ego sum Rufria Amabilis fratri karissimo suo faciendum curavit
EDCS-10000578.translation: To the Divine Shades. Ulpius Clemens lived for two years; he is laid here. May the earth be light for you. I was what you are; you will be what I am. Rufria Amabilis had this erected for her dearest brother.
EDCS-10000578.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from the Imperial period, marks the tomb of Ulpius Clemens, who died at the age of two, and was dedicated by his sister, Rufria Amabilis, including a common memento mori sentiment.

EDCS-10000580.text_edition: annos LX ice coniux etetelix et Herculanus fili et Zosimus lib(ertus) et conhIeres#conheres#CONIERE monumentum posuerunt hic situs est
EDCS-10000580.translation: Ice the spouse, along with Etelix, Herculanus the son, Zosimus the freedman, and the co-heirs, erected this monument for an individual who lived for 60 years. Here he lies.
EDCS-10000580.summary: This funerary monument was erected by the deceased's spouse Ice, along with Etelix, his son Herculanus, his freedman Zosimus, and his co-heirs, for an unnamed man who lived for 60 years.

EDCS-10000589.text_edition: IC  Marg l Valenus domo a |(centurio) legionis
EDCS-10000589.translation: I.C. (Inscribed as below) Valenus from his home, a centurion of the legion.
EDCS-10000589.summary: This inscription identifies Valenus as a centurion of a legion, possibly on a military monument or epitaph, with his origin indicated.

EDCS-10000590.translation: Sacred to Atta. Melior fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-10000590.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription where Melior fulfills a vow made to Atta, likely a deity or revered individual, suggesting a religious offering.

EDCS-10000591.text_edition: Deo Mundryto Claudius Rufus votum solvit
EDCS-10000591.translation: To the god Mundrytus, Claudius Rufus gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10000591.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription from Claudius Rufus, fulfilling a vow to the god Mundrytus, indicating a religious devotion.

EDCS-10000593.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Caius Flavius Felix veteranus legionis VII Claudiae
EDCS-10000593.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Gaius Flavius Felix, a veteran of the Seventh Claudian Legion.
EDCS-10000593.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Gaius Flavius Felix, identifying him as a veteran of Legion VII Claudia, likely an altar or monument from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10000596.text_edition: Tiberius Claudius Valerius veteranus cohortis I Cretum domo Hierapytna vixit annos LX emeruit annos XXXII
EDCS-10000596.translation: Tiberius Claudius Valerius, a veteran of the First Cohort of Cretans, from Hierapytna, lived for 60 years and served for 32 years.
EDCS-10000596.summary: This funerary inscription records the life and military service of Tiberius Claudius Valerius, a veteran of the First Cohort of Cretans from Hierapytna, detailing his age at death and years of service.

EDCS-10000598.text_edition: Auarelius Firmus pro salute sua Herculi posuit
EDCS-10000598.translation: Auarelius Firmus placed this for his own well-being to Hercules.
EDCS-10000598.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription by Auarelius Firmus to Hercules for his own health and safety, suggesting a votive offering.

EDCS-10000600.translation: Aelius Saturninus gave this gift to Omphala.
EDCS-10000600.summary: This inscription records a gift given by Aelius Saturninus to Omphala, possibly a dedicatory or honorific monument.

EDCS-10000603.text_edition: Flavius Lucius et Flavius Lucillus Deo Sancto Laeto Libero Patri pro salutes sic suas sic et vicanorum posuerunt
EDCS-10000603.translation: Flavius Lucius and Flavius Lucillus dedicated this to the Holy God Laetus Liber Pater for the well-being of both themselves and the villagers.
EDCS-10000603.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Flavius Lucius and Flavius Lucillus to the Holy God Laetus Liber Pater for the well-being of themselves and the villagers.

EDCS-10000605.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani nepos divi Nervae pronepos pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul II milia passuum II
EDCS-10000605.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, in his second year of tribunician power, consul for the second time, two miles.
EDCS-10000605.summary: This inscription is a milestone recording the full name and titles of Emperor Antoninus Pius, including his imperial ancestry, pontifical office, and consular and tribunician powers, dated to 139 CE, and indicating a distance of two miles.

EDCS-10000607.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Marco Aemilio Aemiliano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli proconsuli milia passuum XIII
EDCS-10000607.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aemilius Aemilianus, Pious, Fortunate, Undefeated, Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of his country, consul, proconsul. 13 miles.
EDCS-10000607.summary: This inscription is a milestone dedicated to the Emperor Marcus Aemilius Aemilianus in 253 AD, detailing his imperial titles and indicating a distance of 13 miles.

EDCS-10100168.text_edition: Dis Manibus Ulpio Attico et Ulpiae Ianuariae et Ulpio Thalaso Pasades uxor marito et filiae et naepoti sic bene meritis posuit
EDCS-10100168.translation: To the Divine Manes. Pasades, a wife, placed this monument for her husband Ulpius Atticus, and for her daughter Ulpia Ianuaria, and for her grandson Ulpius Thalasus, who thus well deserved it.
EDCS-10100168.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a monument by Pasades, a wife, for her deceased husband Ulpius Atticus, daughter Ulpia Ianuaria, and grandson Ulpius Thalasus, praising them as well-deserving.

EDCS-10100169.text_edition: Dis Manibus Flavia Iucunda vixit annos XL et Super filius ipsius eius sic vixit nnos XX Flavius Terentinus coniugi et filio posuit
EDCS-10100169.translation: To the Divine Shades. Flavia Iucunda lived 40 years, and Super, her son, likewise lived 20 years. Flavius Terentinus placed this for his wife and son.
EDCS-10100169.summary: This is a funerary inscription (likely from a tombstone) dedicated by Flavius Terentinus to his wife Flavia Iucunda, who lived 40 years, and their son Super, who lived 20 years.

EDCS-10100315.text_edition: Dis Manibus Marci Aureli  vixit annos LX
EDCS-10100315.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Aurelius lived for 60 years.
EDCS-10100315.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Manes (Spirits of the Departed) for Marcus Aurelius, stating his age at death.

EDCS-10100319.text_edition: Soli Invicto Valerius Iucundus ex voto posuit  Perpetuo et CornelTiano#Corneliano#CORNETIANO consulibus
EDCS-10100319.translation: To Sol Invictus, Valerius Iucundus erected this in fulfillment of a vow, during the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelianus.
EDCS-10100319.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication made to Sol Invictus by Valerius Iucundus, dated to the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelianus (237 AD).

EDCS-10100399.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely naming Marcus Aurelius, possibly from a funerary or honorific monument, with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-10100400.text_edition: Dis Manibus P(ublius) Prusius Maximus vixit annos XLV Iul(ius) Hieronimus secundus heres et Ulpius Maxumus sic iuxta voluntatem testamenti pecunia sua
EDCS-10100400.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Publius Prusius Maximus lived 45 years. Julius Hieronimus, second heir, and Ulpius Maximus, thus according to the wishes of the will, [erected this] with their own money.
EDCS-10100400.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Prusius Maximus, who lived 45 years, erected by his second heir, Julius Hieronimus, and Ulpius Maximus, using their own funds in accordance with the deceased's will.

EDCS-10100402.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-10100402.text_edition: Dassianus senior et Galla posueruerunt titulo sic pueri sic bimato Dassiani sic iuniori  Dassianus iunior Christi electus neofitus
EDCS-10100402.translation: Dassianus the Elder and Galla placed this inscription for the boy, for Dassianus the Younger, who was two years old. Dassianus the Younger, chosen by Christ, a neophyte.
EDCS-10100402.summary: This Christian funerary inscription, likely from the Late Roman Empire, was erected by Dassianus the Elder and Galla for their two-year-old son, Dassianus the Younger, who is described as a neophyte chosen by Christ.

EDCS-10100405.text_edition: Iunoni Reginae Ulpius Antonius Quintus decurio aedilicius quaestor municipii Diernensium ob honorem quinquennalitatis portus
EDCS-10100405.translation: To Juno the Queen, Ulpius Antonius Quintus, a decurion, former aedile, and quaestor of the municipality of Dierna, dedicated a port on account of the honor of his quinquennal magistracy.
EDCS-10100405.summary: This inscription records Ulpius Antonius Quintus, a municipal official from Dierna, dedicating a port to Juno Regina in recognition of his prestigious quinquennal magistracy.

EDCS-10100407.text_edition: Dis Manibus Iulio Ingenuo veterano cohortis III Campestris vixit annos L Noviia Cassia coniugi virginio pientissimo et sibi fecit vol
EDCS-10100407.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Iulius Ingenuus, a veteran of the Third Campestrian Cohort, who lived 50 years. Noviia Cassia made this for her most devoted husband, Virginius, and for herself. Willingly.
EDCS-10100407.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely on a tombstone or altar, dedicated by Noviia Cassia to her deceased husband, Iulius Ingenuus (also referred to as Virginius), a veteran of the Third Campestrian Cohort who lived 50 years, with the wife also reserving the monument for herself, probably dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-10100408.text_edition: ob salutem Titus Aelius Respectus Nescenses votum libens solvit
EDCS-10100408.translation: For safety, Titus Aelius Respectus Nescenses gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10100408.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription by Titus Aelius Respectus Nescenses, who fulfilled a vow for safety, indicating a religious offering.

EDCS-10100411.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead, with a partial name of the deceased.

EDCS-10100412.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Minerva Augusta, likely made to the imperial cult.

EDCS-10100415.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aelius Iucundus hic situs vixit nnos III dies VIII Aelius Felix pater et Co Marcellina mater
EDCS-10100415.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aelius Iucundus lies here. He lived 3 years and 8 days. Aelius Felix, his father, and Co. Marcellina, his mother.
EDCS-10100415.summary: This funerary inscription records the premature death of Aelius Iucundus, who lived for 3 years and 8 days, and was dedicated by his parents, Aelius Felix and Co. Marcellina, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-10100416.text_edition: Herculano vixit nnos VIIII hic siti sunt Aelia Hesperis mater filiis piissimis faciendum curavit
EDCS-10100416.translation: Herculanus lived for 9 years. Here they are buried. Aelia Hesperis, their mother, took care to have this made for her most dutiful sons.
EDCS-10100416.summary: This funerary inscription records Aelia Hesperis, a mother, dedicating a tombstone to her most dutiful sons, one of whom, Herculanus, died at the age of nine.

EDCS-10100418.text_edition: Dis Manibus Caius Annius Cai libertus Ursio vixit annis XXXXV Annia Fortunata coniugi bene merito fecit
EDCS-10100418.translation: To the Divine Manes. Gaius Annius Ursio, freedman of Gaius, lived for 45 years. Annia Fortunata made this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-10100418.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, dedicated by Annia Fortunata to her husband, Gaius Annius Ursio, a freedman, who died at the age of 45.

EDCS-10100419.text_edition: hic sita est et Aureliae Naneae Aurelius Maximinus filiae bene merenti et coniugi vivae posuit
EDCS-10100419.translation: Here lies Aurelia Nanea. Aurelius Maximinus placed this for his well-deserving daughter and for his wife while she was alive.
EDCS-10100419.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording Aurelia Nanea, set up by Aurelius Maximinus for his daughter and his wife, suggesting a shared monument or a dedication anticipating his own demise.

EDCS-10100420.text_edition: Dis Manibus Corn(elia)? Secunda Valerianaus tesarissima fe haec vixit annos  menses VIII dies XI Valerianae coniugi  mer
EDCS-10100420.translation: To the Divine Manes. Cornelia (?) Secunda, a very diligent 'tesarissima', made this. She lived for [---] years, 8 months, 11 days. For his well-deserving wife Valeriana.
EDCS-10100420.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes for Cornelia (?) Secunda, indicating her age at death, and was likely set up by her husband Valeriana.

EDCS-10100426.text_edition: Iovi Optimo sacrum ex viso Marcus Ulpius Viator beneficiarius consularis ex frumentario
EDCS-10100426.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus, by vision, Marcus Ulpius Viator, a beneficiarius consularis (from the frumentarii).
EDCS-10100426.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus, made by Marcus Ulpius Viator, a consular beneficiarius, after a divine vision, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-10100427.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Minervae sacrum Aurelius Saevinus Proculus beneficiarius consularis votum libens posuit
EDCS-10100427.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, and Minerva. Aurelius Saevinus Proculus, a beneficiarius consularis, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10100427.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Capitoline Triad by Aurelius Saevinus Proculus, a consular beneficiarius, in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-10100428.text_edition: Nemesi reginae sacrum pro salutem sic dominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum Caius Valerius Valens  beneficiarius consularis legionis IIII Flaviae votum libens laetus merito posuit
EDCS-10100428.translation: Sacred to Nemesis Regina, for the health of our lords the Augusti. Gaius Valerius Valens, a beneficiarius consularis of the Fourth Flavian Legion, willingly, joyfully, and deservedly placed this vow.
EDCS-10100428.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Nemesis Regina for the health of the reigning emperors (Augusti) by Gaius Valerius Valens, a consular beneficiarius of Legio IIII Flavia, likely dating to the early-mid 3rd century AD.

EDCS-10100429.text_edition: Nemesi reginae sacrum Ulpius Martialis beneficiarius consularis legionis IIII Flaviae votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10100429.translation: Sacred to Nemesis Regina. Ulpius Martialis, a beneficiarius consularis of the Fourth Flavian Legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10100429.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Nemesis Regina by Ulpius Martialis, a consular beneficiarius of Legio IIII Flavia, in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-10100430.text_edition: Dis Manibus Claudia Bulbita hic sita est Lucius O Tr coniugi bene merenti faciundum curavit
EDCS-10100430.translation: To the Divine Manes. Claudia Bulbita lies here. Lucius O. Tr. saw to the making of this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-10100430.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes for Claudia Bulbita, arranged by her husband Lucius O. Tr.

EDCS-10100431.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Capitolino Titus Aelius Avitus decurio municipii Ulpianorum votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10100431.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Capitolinus. Titus Aelius Avitus, a decurion of the municipium Ulpianorum, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10100431.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Capitolinus by Titus Aelius Avitus, a decurion of the municipium Ulpianorum, in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-10100432.text_edition: Have mihi domina et dulcissima kara sic coniunx amantissima? ---
EDCS-10100432.translation: Farewell to me, mistress and sweetest dear, thus most loving spouse [---]
EDCS-10100432.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription, likely expressing the grief of a spouse.

EDCS-10100433.text_edition: Dis Manibus Valens Turelli vixit annos LXXX Publius Aelius Turellius nepos et heres bene merenti posuit
EDCS-10100433.translation: To the Divine Manes. Valens Turelli lived for 80 years. Publius Aelius Turellius, his grandson and heir, placed this for his well-deserving relative.
EDCS-10100433.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes for Valens Turelli, who lived to 80 years, and was erected by his grandson and heir, Publius Aelius Turellius.

EDCS-10100435.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Z Aelius Cosinus decurio libens posuit
EDCS-10100435.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Z. Aelius Cosinus, decurion, willingly placed this.
EDCS-10100435.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, set up by Z. Aelius Cosinus, a decurion, in fulfillment of a vow, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-10100436.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Varius Crescens
EDCS-10100436.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Varius Crescens.
EDCS-10100436.summary: This inscription is a sacred dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Varius Crescens.

EDCS-10100438.text_edition: Dis deabusque Marcus Aurelius Marcianus beneficiarius consularis legionis VII Claudiae Severianae Alexandrianae votum libens persolvit Agricola et Clementino consulibus
EDCS-10100438.translation: To the gods and goddesses. Marcus Aurelius Marcianus, a beneficiarius consularis of the Seventh Claudian Legion Severiana Alexandriana, willingly paid his vow during the consulship of Agricola and Clementinus.
EDCS-10100438.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to all gods and goddesses by Marcus Aurelius Marcianus, a consular beneficiarius of Legio VII Claudia Severiana Alexandriana, dating to 230 AD during the consulship of Agricola and Clementinus.

EDCS-10100440.text_edition: Deabus sacrum virgines sic Gaudens Liviae servus pro salute pecunia sua et suis
EDCS-10100440.translation: Sacred to the goddesses, the virgins. Gaudens, a slave of Livia, for his own health and that of his family, with his own money.
EDCS-10100440.summary: This inscription is a dedication to unnamed goddesses, likely nymphs or other local deities, made by Gaudens, a slave of Livia, on behalf of himself and his family, using his own funds.

EDCS-10100441.text_edition: ni Ulpia Proculiana vixit annis XXX Lucius Avillius Pudens filius parentibus pientissimis faciendum curavit
EDCS-10100441.translation: Ulpia Proculiana lived for 30 years. Lucius Avillius Pudens, her son, saw to the erection of this monument for his most devoted parents.
EDCS-10100441.summary: This is a funerary inscription, where Lucius Avillius Pudens dedicated a monument to his parents, Ulpia Proculiana and presumably her husband, after Ulpia's death.

EDCS-10100442.translation: Marcus Ulpius [---], decurion of the municipality, aedile.
EDCS-10100442.summary: This is a fragmentary public inscription detailing the offices held by Marcus Ulpius, who served as a decurion and aedile within a municipality, likely dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10100444.text_edition: Dis Manibus Caius Valerius Marullus vixit annos LXXV Caius Valerius Varanus et Nanea Galli sibi vive sic faciendum curaverunt
EDCS-10100444.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Valerius Marullus lived for 75 years. Gaius Valerius Varanus and Nanea Galli, for themselves while living, saw to the erection of this monument.
EDCS-10100444.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the departed, commemorating Gaius Valerius Marullus and prepared by Gaius Valerius Varanus and Nanea Galli for themselves during their lifetime.

EDCS-10100445.text_edition: cum qua felicem vixit vitam annis XXX Caius Avillius Pudens filius parentibus pientissimis faciendum curavit t Ulpiano hoc sepulcrum heredem non sequetur
EDCS-10100445.translation: With whom he lived a happy life for 30 years. Gaius Avillius Pudens, son, saw to the erection of this monument for his most devoted parents. This sepulchre will not pass to an heir concerning Ulpianus.
EDCS-10100445.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Gaius Avillius Pudens dedicated a monument to his parents, stating that the tomb will not be inherited by Ulpianus's heir, implying a specific testamentary clause.

EDCS-10100446.text_edition: Genio Illyrici  pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Severi Alexandri Augusti et Mamaeae Augustae sub cura Antoni Silvani viri egregii procuratoris Iulianus vilicus stationis Ulpianensis tabularius sumptu suo fe=ILcit#fecit#ILC Albino et Maximo consulibus
EDCS-10100446.translation: To the Genius of Illyricum, for the welfare of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Augustus and Mamaea Augusta, under the care of Antonius Silvanus, excellent man, procurator. Iulianus, estate manager of the Ulpian station, accountant, made this at his own expense in the consulship of Albinus and Maximus.
EDCS-10100446.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to the Genius of Illyricum, made for the welfare of Emperor Severus Alexander and Mamaea Augusta by Iulianus, a financial official, in 227 CE.

EDCS-10100448.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Imperatorum Augustorum Victor Demetri et Demetrius Ambibi sacerdotes eiusdem dei solverunt votum
EDCS-10100448.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the welfare of our lords the Emperors Augusti. Victor Demetrius and Demetrius Ambibus, priests of the same god, fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-10100448.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, offered by two priests, Victor Demetrius and Demetrius Ambibus, for the welfare of the reigning emperors.

EDCS-10100449.text_edition: Furiae Titi filiae Varanillae clarissimae feminae sanctissimus ordo municipii Ulpiani ob multa eius bene merita basim et statuam vexatam Furia Caecilia clarissima femina restituit
EDCS-10100449.translation: To Furia Varanilla, daughter of Titus, most distinguished woman. The most sacred order of the Ulpian municipality, because of her many merits, had a vexed base and statue. Furia Caecilia, most distinguished woman, restored it.
EDCS-10100449.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Furia Varanilla, a distinguished woman, by the Ulpian municipality, with a restoration of her base and statue carried out by Furia Caecilia.

EDCS-10100450.text_edition: Marco Pontio Marci filio Pupinia Varano Sabino viro clarissimo tribuno legionis III Augustae quaestori tribuno plebis sanctissimus ordo municipii Ulpianensium ob multa in rem publicam eius merita basim et statuam vextatam Furia Caecilia clarissima femina restituit
EDCS-10100450.translation: To Marcus Pontius Varanus Sabinus, son of Marcus, of the Pupinia tribe, a most distinguished man, tribune of the Third Augustan Legion, quaestor, tribune of the plebs. The most sacred order of the Ulpian municipality, because of his many merits towards the state, had a vexed base and statue. Furia Caecilia, most distinguished woman, restored it.
EDCS-10100450.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Marcus Pontius Varanus Sabinus, a distinguished man with a significant military and political career, by the Ulpian municipality, with a restoration of his base and statue by Furia Caecilia.

EDCS-10100452.summary: This inscription records a dedication or monument placed by Celer, who had served as duumvir twice, indicating his prominent public role.

EDCS-10100457.text_edition: Zbeltiurdi Sextius Ba-pecus optio pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10100457.translation: Zbeltiurdi Sextius Ba-pecus, optio, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for his own welfare and that of his family.
EDCS-10100457.summary: This is a votive inscription where Sextius Ba-pecus, an optio (military officer), fulfilled a vow for his own and his family's well-being.

EDCS-10100458.text_edition: Dis Manibus Ulpius Valentinus vixit annis XV Ulpius Andinus et Aurelia Iulia parentes filio pientissimo et sibi vivis merentibus posuerunt
EDCS-10100458.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Ulpius Valentinus lived for 15 years. Ulpius Andinus and Aurelia Iulia, his parents, placed this for their most devoted son and for themselves while living and deserving.
EDCS-10100458.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the departed, erected by Ulpius Andinus and Aurelia Iulia for their deceased 15-year-old son, Ulpius Valentinus, and also for themselves during their lifetime.

EDCS-10100459.text_edition: Nymphis Lucius Titovius Pro votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-10100459.translation: To the Nymphs. Lucius Titovius Pro fulfilled his vow willingly, deservedly.
EDCS-10100459.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to the Nymphs, fulfilled by Lucius Titovius Pro as a deserved act.

EDCS-10100460.text_edition: et Sitae filio Sumi corniculario cohortis primae Dardanorum vixit annos XXXX bene merenti posuit
EDCS-10100460.translation: And to Sita, son of Sumus, cornicularius of the First Cohort of the Dardani, who lived for 40 years. This was placed for him, who was well-deserving.
EDCS-10100460.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Sita, son of Sumus, a cornicularius (military clerk) of the First Cohort of the Dardani, who died at the age of 40.

EDCS-10100462.text_edition: Dis Manibus Valens Timentis nnorum XXXV sibi et Cittuni suae superstiti bene merenti posuit
EDCS-10100462.translation: To the Divine Manes. Valens, son of Timentis, aged 35 years, placed this for himself and for his deserving wife Cittunis, still living.
EDCS-10100462.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valens, dedicated by himself and his still-living wife Cittunis, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-10100463.text_edition: Asclepio  Telesphoro  Hygiae  So  Heracliti Surus et pro Genio Dolicheni paterno Deo et Genio cohortis votum libies sic fecit
EDCS-10100463.translation: To Asclepius, Telesphorus, Hygia, Sol, Heraclitus. Surus, also for the paternal genius of Dolichenus, god, and the genius of the cohort, willingly made this vow.
EDCS-10100463.summary: This is a religious dedication by Surus to several deities, including Asclepius, Telesphorus, Hygia, Sol, Heraclitus, and the genii of Dolichenus and a cohort, likely a military or private votive altar.

EDCS-10100465.text_edition: Dis Manibus Septimia Pitta vixit nnos XL Dunnus pater filiae carae posuit
EDCS-10100465.translation: To the Divine Manes. Septimia Pitta lived 40 years. Dunnus, her father, placed this for his dear daughter.
EDCS-10100465.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead, erected by Dunnus for his beloved daughter Septimia Pitta, who lived 40 years.

EDCS-10100466.text_edition: Dis Manibus Lucio Silvio Cai Praeceptori qui vixit et Andiae matri vivae posuerunt Lucius Silvius Italicus filius cum coheredibus
EDCS-10100466.translation: To the Divine Manes. Lucius Silvius Italicus, the son, with co-heirs, placed this for Lucius Silvius, son of Gaius, his tutor, who lived, and for his mother Andia, while she is still alive.
EDCS-10100466.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Lucius Silvius Italicus and co-heirs to Lucius Silvius, their tutor, and to Andia, their mother, while she was still alive, indicating a family tomb.

EDCS-10100469.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Ingenuus beneficiarius consularis legionis VII Claudiae votum solvit libens
EDCS-10100469.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Publius Aelius Ingenuus, a beneficiarius consularis of the Seventh Legion Claudia, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-10100469.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Publius Aelius Ingenuus, a beneficiarius consularis of Legio VII Claudia, likely an altar erected upon fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-10100470.text_edition: Dis Manibus Ulpius Cassius Ulpi Aeli liberto vixit nnos XXVI et matri Cinna filio et uxori se vivo posuit
EDCS-10100470.translation: To the Divine Manes. Ulpius Cassius, freedman of Ulpius Aelius, lived 26 years. Cinna, his mother, placed this for her son and for his wife, during her own lifetime.
EDCS-10100470.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Cinna for her son, Ulpius Cassius, a freedman, and for his wife, during Cinna's lifetime, commemorating a young man who died at age 26.

EDCS-10100471.translation: To Aesculapius, Hygia, and Telesphorus. Thus.
EDCS-10100471.summary: This is a religious dedication to the healing deities Aesculapius, Hygia, and Telesphorus, likely made on an altar or votive object, with no dedicant named or date specified.

EDCS-10100472.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aper milis sic exercitus vixit annis XXXV militavit annis XIII Ursus cuntubernalis sic cuntubernali sic bene merito titulum posuit
EDCS-10100472.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aper, a soldier from the army, lived 35 years and served for 13 years. Ursus, his tent-mate, placed this inscription for his well-deserving tent-mate.
EDCS-10100472.summary: This is a military funerary inscription dedicated by Ursus to his tent-mate, Aper, a soldier who died at 35 after 13 years of service.

EDCS-10100473.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aelius Maurus Kandido sic karo sic filio suo et sibi et coniugi
EDCS-10100473.translation: To the spirits of the dead. Aelius Maurus for Candidus, his dear son, and for himself and his wife. Thus.
EDCS-10100473.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a tomb erected by Aelius Maurus for his beloved son Candidus, and also for himself and his wife, a common practice in Roman burial monuments.

EDCS-10100474.text_edition: Aelius Niceforus libertus ipse sibi et Baebiae PS coniugi
EDCS-10100474.translation: Aelius Niceforus, a freedman, for himself and Baebia P.S., his wife.
EDCS-10100474.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a tomb built by Aelius Niceforus, a freedman, for himself and his wife Baebia P.S., indicating a private burial monument.

EDCS-10100475.text_edition: Dis Manibus Rufinus Dassi pr et Andiae Dassi coniugi memoriam sibi vivi posuerunt sta dum venio memoria superis vixit nnos LX
EDCS-10100475.translation: To the Divine Manes. Rufinus, son of Dassus, and Andia, wife of Dassus, placed this memorial for themselves while living. 'Stop, while I come to the memory of those above.' He lived 60 years.
EDCS-10100475.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Rufinus and Andia, set up by themselves during their lifetimes, including a common epigraphic plea to passersby and stating Rufinus lived 60 years.

EDCS-10100476.translation: Ulpius Aper erected this for himself while alive.
EDCS-10100476.summary: This is a funerary inscription indicating that Ulpius Aper had a tomb or monument built for himself during his lifetime, a practice known as 'v.s.p.' (vivus sibi posuit).

EDCS-10100480.text_edition: Mercurio Marcus Ulpius Severianus libens merito posuit
EDCS-10100480.translation: To Mercury. Marcus Ulpius Severianus willingly and deservedly erected this.
EDCS-10100480.summary: This is a religious dedication made by Marcus Ulpius Severianus to the god Mercury, indicating a votive offering or altar, likely dating to the Imperial period given the 'Ulpius' nomen.

EDCS-10100487.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Pio Felici invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate  consuli
EDCS-10100487.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, consul.
EDCS-10100487.summary: This is an honorific inscription dedicated to Emperor Decius, listing his full name and imperial titles, likely from a statue base or triumphal arch, dating to 249-251 AD.
