EDCS-11301517.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple to Diana Augusta by Marcus Aurelius Quadratianus, a tribune, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-11301518.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno Flavius Bitus DD ex voto posuit
EDCS-11301518.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paternus. Flavius Bitus, by gift of the gods, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301518.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paternus, set up by Flavius Bitus as a fulfilled vow, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11301521.text_edition: Marti equitum pro salute dominorum nnn(ostrorum) Severi et Antonini impperatorum Gaius Atrius Decoratus tribunus cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum Antoninianae |(miliariae) equitatae libens posuit
EDCS-11301521.translation: To Mars of the horsemen, for the welfare of our lords, the emperors Severus and Antoninus, Gaius Atrius Decoratus, tribune of the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, Antoninian, a thousand strong, mounted, willingly placed this.
EDCS-11301521.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Mars by Gaius Atrius Decoratus, a tribune, for the welfare of Emperors Severus and Antoninus, likely dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301523.text_edition: Invicto Deo Castinus votum libens posuit
EDCS-11301523.translation: To the Unconquered God. Castinus willingly set up his vow.
EDCS-11301523.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to an 'Unconquered God,' likely Sol Invictus or Mithras, set up by Castinus willingly in fulfillment of a vow, probably from the later Imperial period.

EDCS-11301524.text_edition: Invicto deo pro salute Impperatorum Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Luci Aureli Veri [Augusti &
EDCS-11301524.translation: To the Invincible god, for the welfare of the emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus.
EDCS-11301524.summary: This inscription is a dedication to an Invincible god for the welfare of co-emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla) and Lucius Aurelius Verus, indicating a date between 161-169 AD if referring to the earlier Verus, or the early 3rd century AD if referring to Caracalla alongside another Antoninus.

EDCS-11301525.text_edition: pro reditu et victoria RV  Pii Felicis Augusti anus EXVIIVL  Iulianum L  Valens V sacerdotes MRIO  VIM AELOS
EDCS-11301525.translation: For the return and victory of [---] Pius Felix Augustus, an old woman [---] Julianus [---] Valens, priests [---].
EDCS-11301525.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a dedication for the return and victory of an emperor, likely bearing the title Pius Felix Augustus, by priests.

EDCS-11301526.text_edition: Pro salute Imperatoris Flavius Clemes Sabazio Paterno Augusto posuit Marco Antonio Gordiano Augusto et Clodio Pompeiano consulibus
EDCS-11301526.translation: For the welfare of the Emperor, Flavius Clemes placed this to Sabazius Paternus Augustus, during the consulship of Marcus Antonius Gordianus Augustus and Clodius Pompeianus.
EDCS-11301526.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Sabazius Paternus Augustus by Flavius Clemes for the Emperor's welfare, dated to the consulship of Marcus Antonius Gordianus Augustus and Clodius Pompeianus, which corresponds to 241 AD.

EDCS-11301527.text_edition: Titus Aelius Mucianus et Tullia Augusta ex voto posuerunt
EDCS-11301527.translation: Titus Aelius Mucianus and Tullia Augusta set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-11301527.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Titus Aelius Mucianus and Tullia Augusta in accordance with a vow.

EDCS-11301528.text_edition: Trevius sic Salustianus sic votum posuit
EDCS-11301528.translation: Thus Trevius, thus Salustianus, placed this vow.
EDCS-11301528.summary: This inscription records a vow placed by Trevius and Salustianus, likely as a dedicatory act.

EDCS-11301532.text_edition: pro salute dominorum nnnostrorum Severi et Antonini Impperatorum Caius Atrius Decoratus tribunus cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum Antoninianae Mmiliariae equitatae libens posuit
EDCS-11301532.translation: For the welfare of our lords, the emperors Severus and Antoninus, Gaius Atrius Decoratus, tribune of the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, Antoninian, a thousand strong, mounted, willingly placed this.
EDCS-11301532.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Gaius Atrius Decoratus, a military tribune, to an unnamed deity for the welfare of Emperors Severus and Antoninus, likely from the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301534.translation: For the health of Lucius Septimius Severus and [---]
EDCS-11301534.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary dedication made for the well-being of the emperor Lucius Septimius Severus, suggesting a date between 193-211 AD.

EDCS-11301535.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici invicto Augusto tribunicia potestate V cons(uli) II patri patriae proconsuli cohors II Aurelia Dardanorum Gordiana devota numini maiestatique eius dedicante Lucio Catio Celere legato Augusti pro praetore Attico et Praetextato consulibus
EDCS-11301535.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix, invincible Augustus, with tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of his country, proconsul; the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, Gordian, devoted to his divinity and majesty, dedicated this, with Lucius Catius Celer, legate of Augustus with praetorian rank, dedicating it, during the consulship of Atticus and Praetextatus.
EDCS-11301535.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, Gordian, to Emperor Gordian III, specifying his imperial titles and dating the event to his 5th tribunician power and 2nd consulship (241 AD), under the dedication of Lucius Catius Celer and during the consulship of Atticus and Praetextatus.

EDCS-11301536.text_edition: ON Clementinus ornatus ornamentis decurionalibus coloniae Ratiariae amico bene merenti posuit
EDCS-11301536.translation: [---] Clementinus, adorned with the decurional ornaments of the colony of Ratiaria, placed this for a well-deserving friend.
EDCS-11301536.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument or honorary dedication by Clementinus, a decurion from the colony of Ratiaria, to a well-deserving friend.

EDCS-11301537.text_edition: Dis Manibus Valerius Nico decurio M municipi  vixit annos LXXV Valeria Nicomedia filia et Publius Valerius Gelasio
EDCS-11301537.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valerius Nico, decurion of the municipality, lived 75 years. Valeria Nicomedia, his daughter, and Publius Valerius Gelasio [set this up].
EDCS-11301537.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument dedicated to Valerius Nico, a decurion who lived to 75, set up by his daughter Valeria Nicomedia and Publius Valerius Gelasio.

EDCS-11301538.text_edition: Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Augustinus veteranus ex cohorte praetoria vixit annos LXX Aurelius Candidus fratri et Aurelius Surus patri et Maius C
EDCS-11301538.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Aurelius Augustinus, a veteran from the praetorian cohort, lived 70 years. Aurelius Candidus [set this up] for his brother, and Aurelius Surus for his father, and Maius C[---].
EDCS-11301538.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument dedicated to Marcus Aurelius Augustinus, a praetorian veteran who lived for 70 years, set up by his brother Aurelius Candidus, his father Aurelius Surus, and Maius C[---].

EDCS-11301539.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelius Aurelianus miles legionis IIII Flaviae vixit annis XXXV Aurelius Bithus veteranus pater et Aelia Aide mater filio bene merenti et sibi se vivis posuerunt
EDCS-11301539.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelius Aurelianus, a soldier of the Fourth Flavian Legion, lived for 35 years. Aurelius Bithus, his veteran father, and Aelia Aide, his mother, placed this for their well-deserving son and for themselves during their lifetime.
EDCS-11301539.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Aurelianus, a soldier of Legio IIII Flavia, set up by his parents, Aurelius Bithus and Aelia Aide, for their son and for themselves while still living.

EDCS-11301540.text_edition: Aurelius V imaginifer cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum militavit nnos XXII et Aurelia Primitiva coniux eius vixit nnos L Aurelius Valens signifer legionis VII Claudiae patri et matri bene merentibus posuit
EDCS-11301540.translation: Aurelius V, an imaginifer of the Second Aurelian Cohort of the Dardanians, served for 22 years, and Aurelia Primitiva, his wife, lived for 50 years. Aurelius Valens, a standard-bearer of the Seventh Claudian Legion, placed this for his well-deserving father and mother.
EDCS-11301540.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Aurelius Valens, a standard-bearer, to his parents, Aurelius V, an imaginifer of Cohors II Aurelia Dardanorum, and Aurelia Primitiva, his wife.

EDCS-11301542.text_edition: Dis Manibus Ulpius Aquilinus miles legionis VII Claudiae librarius offici praefecti territorii vixit nnos XXII Aquileiensis et Ulpia Diotima filio dulcissimo bene merenti posuerunt
EDCS-11301542.translation: To the Divine Manes. Ulpius Aquilinus, a soldier of the Seventh Claudian Legion, a clerk of the office of the prefect of the territory, lived for 22 years. Aquileiensis and Ulpia Diotima placed this for their sweetest, well-deserving son.
EDCS-11301542.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Ulpius Aquilinus, a soldier and clerk in Legio VII Claudia, set up by his parents, Aquileiensis and Ulpia Diotima, likely around the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301543.text_edition: Ingenuus  legionis VII Claudiae qui vixit nnos XLV militavit nnos XVI semissem interfectus in expeditione Parthica et Aurelius Valentinus qui et Octavius filius heres et Claudia Cocceia mater filio bene merenti posuit ave Ingenue bene valeas viator
EDCS-11301543.translation: Ingenuus, of the Seventh Claudian Legion, who lived for 45 years and served for 16 and a half years, was killed on the Parthian expedition. Aurelius Valentinus, also known as Octavius, his son and heir, and Claudia Cocceia, his mother, placed this for their well-deserving son. Farewell, Ingenuus, may you fare well, traveler.
EDCS-11301543.summary: This is a funerary monument for Ingenuus, a soldier of Legio VII Claudia, who died during the Parthian expedition, erected by his son Aurelius Valentinus and his mother Claudia Cocceia, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301544.text_edition: Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius Vibius |(centurio) legionis X fretensis po
EDCS-11301544.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Ulpius Vibius, centurion of the Tenth Legion Fretensis, set this up.
EDCS-11301544.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead, commemorating Marcus Ulpius Vibius, a centurion of the Legio X Fretensis.

EDCS-11301545.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Aurelius Mercurius miles legionis XIII geminae vixit nnos L militavit nnos XVIIII et Aureli--
EDCS-11301545.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelius Mercurius, a soldier of the Thirteenth Gemina Legion, lived for 50 years, served for 19 years, and Aureli[---]
EDCS-11301545.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Aurelius Mercurius, a soldier of Legio XIII Gemina, set up by an Aureli[---], likely a family member.

EDCS-11301546.text_edition: Dis Manibus Quintus Valerius Germanus veteranus cohortis I Thracum militavit annos XXXII Ma  posuit
EDCS-11301546.translation: To the Divine Manes. Quintus Valerius Germanus, a veteran of the First Thracian Cohort, served for 32 years. Ma[---] placed this.
EDCS-11301546.summary: This is a funerary monument for Quintus Valerius Germanus, a veteran of Cohors I Thracum, erected by an individual named Ma[---].

EDCS-11301547.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelius Marcus eques Romanus vixit annos X et Aurelius Marcianus vixit annos VII Aurelius Martianus vir egregius praepositus cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum pater et Aurelia Donata mater et Aurelia Severina soror se sic superviventes filiis bene merentibus posuerunt
EDCS-11301547.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelius Marcus, a Roman knight, lived for 10 years, and Aurelius Marcianus lived for 7 years. Aurelius Martianus, an excellent man, commander of the Second Aurelian Cohort of the Dardanians, their father, and Aurelia Donata, their mother, and Aurelia Severina, their sister, placed this for their well-deserving sons, surviving them in this manner.
EDCS-11301547.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Aurelius Marcus and Aurelius Marcianus, set up by their father Aurelius Martianus, a distinguished commander of Cohors II Aurelia Dardanorum, their mother Aurelia Donata, and their sister Aurelia Severina.

EDCS-11301548.text_edition: Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Salvianus |(centurio) cohortis II Aureliae vixit nnos XLI militavit nnos XXI et Aurelia Salviane sic filia vixit nnos V menses VIIII Cocceia Petilia coniugi et filiae et sibi se viva bene merentibus posuit
EDCS-11301548.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Aurelius Salvianus, a centurion of the Second Aurelian Cohort, lived for 41 years and served for 21 years, and Aurelia Salviane, his daughter, lived for 5 years and 9 months. Cocceia Petilia, while still alive, placed this for her well-deserving husband and daughter, and for herself.
EDCS-11301548.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Cocceia Petilia for her husband, Marcus Aurelius Salvianus, a centurion of Cohors II Aurelia, and their daughter Aurelia Salviane, as well as for herself, while she was still alive.

EDCS-11301549.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Flavio Bitho adiutori cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum marito et Aurelio Hermogeni decurioni cohortis supra scriptae fratri Aurelia Cristina posuit
EDCS-11301549.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelia Cristina placed this for Flavius Bithus, an adjutant of the Second Aurelian Cohort of the Dardanians, her husband, and for Aurelius Hermogenes, a decurion of the above-mentioned cohort, her brother.
EDCS-11301549.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Aurelia Cristina to her husband, Flavius Bithus, an adjutant, and her brother, Aurelius Hermogenes, a decurion, both serving in Cohors II Aurelia Dardanorum.

EDCS-11301550.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Titus Flavius Maximus veteranus ex decurione cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum vixit annos LXX et Flavius Honoratus filius eius vixit annos XXV Aurelia Crescentilla marito et Flavias sic Maximilla Magnilla Quinta patri et fratri bene merentibus posuerunt
EDCS-11301550.translation: To the Divine Manes. Titus Flavius Maximus, a veteran formerly a decurion of the Second Aurelian Cohort of the Dardanians, lived for 70 years, and Flavius Honoratus, his son, lived for 25 years. Aurelia Crescentilla placed this for her husband, and Flavias Maximilla, Magnilla, and Quinta placed it for their well-deserving father and brother.
EDCS-11301550.summary: This is a funerary monument for Titus Flavius Maximus, a veteran and former decurion of Cohors II Aurelia Dardanorum, and his son, Flavius Honoratus, set up by Aurelia Crescentilla, his wife, and his daughters Flavias Maximilla, Magnilla, and Quinta.

EDCS-11301551.text_edition: Dis Manibus Titus Iulius Saturninus veteranus ex decurione cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum sibi vivo et Ovidiae Pudentillae coniugi karissimae cum qua conubio stabili quinquaginta annos sine secessu dulciter egit
EDCS-11301551.publication: IMS-03-02, 00046 = ILJug-03, 01303 = CLEMoes 00018 = CLENuovo p 73 = CLENuovo p 163
EDCS-11301551.translation: To the Divine Manes. Titus Iulius Saturninus, a veteran, formerly a decurion of the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, for himself while living, and for Ovidia Pudentilla, his dearest wife, with whom he sweetly lived for fifty years in a stable marriage, without separation.
EDCS-11301551.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the dedication made by a veteran decurion for himself while alive and for his beloved wife, celebrating their fifty years of marriage.

EDCS-11301552.text_edition: Dis Manibus Valeria Furnia vixit annos XXXVII Rusticianus Valerianus Lucilla Faestus sic matri et Aelius Rusticus decurio duplarius cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum coniugi bene merenti et vivo se sic sibi possuerunt
EDCS-11301552.translation: To the Divine Manes. Valeria Furnia lived for 37 years. Rusticianus Valerianus, Lucilla Faestus, and Aelius Rusticus, a decurion duplarius of the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, placed this for their well-deserving mother and for his well-deserving wife, and for himself while living.
EDCS-11301552.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by a decurion duplarius and others to Valeria Furnia, who died at 37, and also for himself while still living, sometime after the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-11301553.text_edition: Dis Manibus Septimius Super veteranus ex hastiliario cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum vixit annos LX et Septimius Longinus vixit annos VII et Septimia Firmina vixit mensem et Septimius Exuperatus vixit annum I Flavia Maximilla filiis et marito bene merentibus posuit
EDCS-11301553.translation: To the Divine Manes. Septimius Super, a veteran, formerly a hastilarius of the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, lived for 60 years. And Septimius Longinus lived for 7 years. And Septimia Firmina lived for one month. And Septimius Exuperatus lived for 1 year. Flavia Maximilla placed this for her well-deserving sons and husband.
EDCS-11301553.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Flavia Maximilla to her husband, a veteran hastilarius, and their three children, all of whom had died.

EDCS-11301554.text_edition: Dis Manibus Tito Aelio Martiali medico cohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum stipendiorum XXII mater et Aelia Latina
EDCS-11301554.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Titus Aelius Martialis, a doctor of the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, who served for 22 years. His mother and Aelia Latina placed this.
EDCS-11301554.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by his mother and Aelia Latina to Titus Aelius Martialis, a military doctor who served for 22 years, likely from the early to mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301555.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aelia Plotina vixit nnos LX Aurelius Valens et Petilia Surilla coniuges Lucio Egnatio Aristiano Supero militi cohortis II Dardanorum et <K=R>allistiano#<K>allistiano#RALLISTIANO et Neglecto filiis eius
EDCS-11301555.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aelia Plotina lived for 60 years. Aurelius Valens and Petilia Surilla, husband and wife, for Lucius Egnatius Aristianus Superus, a soldier of the Second Cohort of Dardanians, and for Callistianus and Neglectus, his sons.
EDCS-11301555.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aurelius Valens and Petilia Surilla to Aelia Plotina, who lived for 60 years, and also to Lucius Egnatius Aristianus Superus, a soldier, and his sons Callistianus and Neglectus.

EDCS-11301556.text_edition: mens Ccohortis II Aureliae Dardanorum Antoninianae vixit annos XXVIII militavit annos X Flavius Firminus heres iuxta volunPtate#voluntate#VOLUPT INTR die XXX RENE
EDCS-11301556.translation: To the Divine Manes. [---] of the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians, Antoninian. He lived for 28 years, served for 10 years. Flavius Firminus, his heir, placed this according to his will on the 30th day. [---]
EDCS-11301556.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Flavius Firminus, as heir, to a soldier of the Second Aurelian Cohort of Dardanians (Antoninian), who served 10 years and died at age 28, likely from the early to mid-3rd century AD during the Severan dynasty.

EDCS-11301557.text_edition: Dis Inferis Manibus Valerius Iovinus ex ducenario equitum promotorum qui vixit annosaginta duos patrono impromiscuae fidei Nestori sui temporis moribus bene cognito iusto officioso operoso Vibia Sicca et Valeria Marcella et Flavia Iovina ob abstemie nobiliterque facta memoriam fecerunt
EDCS-11301557.translation: To the Infernal and Divine Manes. Valerius Iovinus, formerly a ducenarius of the promoted cavalry, who lived for 82 years. For their patron of unwavering loyalty, Nestor of their time, well-known for his character, just, dutiful, industrious. Vibia Sicca, Valeria Marcella, and Flavia Iovina made this memorial for his noble and temperate deeds.
EDCS-11301557.summary: This is a memorial erected by Vibia Sicca, Valeria Marcella, and Flavia Iovina for their patron, Valerius Iovinus, a former cavalry officer who lived to 82, celebrating his virtuous character.

EDCS-11301559.text_edition: Dis Manibus Trebia Marcellina vixit annis XLV Aurelius Augustinus veteranus coniugi bene merenti posuit
EDCS-11301559.translation: To the Divine Manes. Trebia Marcellina lived for 45 years. Aurelius Augustinus, a veteran, placed this for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-11301559.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Aurelius Augustinus, a veteran, to his well-deserving wife, Trebia Marcellina, who died at age 45.

EDCS-11301560.text_edition: Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Severus veteranus vixit nnos LX et Severianus vixit nnos XX et Crescentilla vixit nnos II Iulia Cassia coniugi et filiis bene merentibus simul et sibi se vivo posuit
EDCS-11301560.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Aurelius Severus, a veteran, lived for 60 years. And Severianus lived for 20 years. And Crescentilla lived for 2 years. Iulia Cassia placed this for her well-deserving husband and sons, and for herself while living.
EDCS-11301560.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Iulia Cassia to her deceased husband, a veteran, and their two sons, and also for herself during her lifetime, likely from the mid-2nd to 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301561.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aurelius Tatius veteranus vixit nnos LX Aurelia Longina coniux una cum filis suis simul et sibi se viva fecit
EDCS-11301561.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelius Tatius, a veteran, lived for 60 years. Aurelia Longina, his wife, together with her sons, made this for him and for herself while living.
EDCS-11301561.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Aurelia Longina, along with her sons, to her husband, Aurelius Tatius, a veteran who lived for 60 years, and also for herself while still alive.

EDCS-11301562.text_edition: Dassius veteranus vixit annos LX bene merenti simul et vivae Aureliae Severae coniugi eius siqut sic testamento suo Aelio Ennio primo heredi intercessione Valeri Aquilini defuncti domi delegavit
EDCS-11301562.translation: Dassius the veteran, who lived for 60 years, for his well-deserving wife Aurelia Severa, who is still alive, just as in his will he delegated to Aelius Ennius, his primary heir, through the intervention of Valerius Aquilinus, deceased in his home.
EDCS-11301562.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely for Dassius, a veteran, made for his living wife Aurelia Severa, mentioning testamentary dispositions involving Aelius Ennius and Valerius Aquilinus, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301563.text_edition: Septimius x veteranus vixit annos LVII et Septimio Testae veterano vixit nnos LX
EDCS-11301563.translation: Septimius X, a veteran, lived for 57 years, and for Septimius Testa, a veteran, lived for 60 years.
EDCS-11301563.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating two veterans, Septimius X and Septimius Testa, likely erected by their families or comrades, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301565.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aelia Crispinilla vixit annis VIII mensibus III Titus Aelius Mucianus et Tullia Augusta filiae dulcissimae simul et sibi se vivi fecerunt et Aeliae Crispinae filiae dulcissimae quae vixit annis IIII mensibus VII
EDCS-11301565.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Crispinilla lived for 8 years and 3 months. Titus Aelius Mucianus and Tullia Augusta made this for their sweetest daughter and for themselves while they are alive, and for Aelia Crispina, their sweetest daughter, who lived for 4 years and 7 months.
EDCS-11301565.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Titus Aelius Mucianus and Tullia Augusta to their two deceased daughters, Aelia Crispinilla and Aelia Crispina, and also for themselves during their lifetimes, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301566.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aelia Gaia vixit nnos XVIII Flavia Longinilla mater et Aelia Longina filia et Aelius Vitalis vivo suo coniugi carissimae bene merenti posuerunt
EDCS-11301566.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Gaia lived for 18 years. Her mother Flavia Longinilla, her daughter Aelia Longina, and Aelius Vitalis placed this for their dearest wife, who deserved well, while he is alive.
EDCS-11301566.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia Gaia, erected by her mother Flavia Longinilla, her daughter Aelia Longina, and her husband Aelius Vitalis during his lifetime, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301567.text_edition: vixit annos XI liber infelicissimus Publius Aelius Satreus bene merenti posuerunt
EDCS-11301567.translation: He lived for 11 years, a most unhappy child. They placed this for Publius Aelius Satreus, who deserved well.
EDCS-11301567.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Satreus, a child who lived only eleven years, erected by his grieving family, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301568.text_edition: Dis Manibus Antonia Veneria viva annorum LIII Masculus Licconis annorum LX se vivo sic sibi bene merentibus posuerunt
EDCS-11301568.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Antonia Veneria, alive at 53 years, and Masculus Licconis, 60 years old. They placed this for themselves while alive, for their well-deserving selves.
EDCS-11301568.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Antonia Veneria and Masculus Licconis for themselves during their lifetimes, likely for their eventual burial place, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301569.text_edition: Dis Manibus Attediae Crescentillae vixit annos XVII et Attediae Caeciliae vixit annos VI Attedius Cresces pater et Valeria mater filiabus posuerunt
EDCS-11301569.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Attedia Crescentilla, who lived for 17 years, and for Attedia Caecilia, who lived for 6 years. Attedius Cresces, the father, and Valeria, the mother, placed this for their daughters.
EDCS-11301569.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Attedius Cresces and Valeria to their two deceased daughters, Attedia Crescentilla and Attedia Caecilia, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301570.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelius Castor [---]
EDCS-11301570.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead, for an individual named Aurelius Castor.

EDCS-11301571.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Marcus Aurelius Exmaragdus sic vixit nnos XXVII et Valeria Valentina vixit nnos XXVIII et Valeria Alexandria vixit nnos XIII Flavia Crispina mater filiis carissimis simul et sibi se sic viva posuit
EDCS-11301571.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Aurelius Exmaragdus lived for 27 years, and Valeria Valentina lived for 28 years, and Valeria Alexandria lived for 13 years. Flavia Crispina, the mother, placed this for her dearest children and for herself while she is alive.
EDCS-11301571.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Flavia Crispina for her three deceased children, Marcus Aurelius Exmaragdus, Valeria Valentina, and Valeria Alexandria, and also for herself while she is still living, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301572.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Aurelius Gaianus vixit annos XX
EDCS-11301572.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurelius Gaianus, who lived for 20 years.
EDCS-11301572.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead, commemorating Aurelius Gaianus, who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-11301573.translation: To the Divine Manes. Claudia Theodosia, Theodosius [---]
EDCS-11301573.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead, mentioning Claudia Theodosia and Theodosius, possibly a family burial.

EDCS-11301574.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Claudius Cassianus tegitur cum coniuge Flavia iunctaque propinquis virgo his Cassia nata sex denos annos vixit pater bisque vicenos mater ter quinos vixit hos usque puella filii et fratres meritis sollemnia reddunt Cassius et Rufus Marinius sororque
EDCS-11301574.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Claudius Cassianus is buried with his wife Flavia, and nearby are their relatives. Cassia, a maiden, lived for sixteen years. The father lived for twice twenty years, and the mother for thrice five years. The children and brothers, Cassius and Rufus Marinius, and their sister, render these solemn rites to their merits.
EDCS-11301574.summary: This is a complex family funerary inscription recording the burial of Claudius Cassianus, his wife Flavia, their maiden daughter Cassia, and likely other unnamed relatives, with commemorative rites performed by Cassius, Rufus Marinius, and their sister, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301576.text_edition: A curaverunt  Cornelius Isidoruster et Flav
EDCS-11301576.translation: A. Cornelius Isidoruster and Flavius took care of this.
EDCS-11301576.summary: This fragmentary inscription records that Cornelius Isidoruster and Flavius oversaw or funded some unspecified work or dedication.

EDCS-11301577.text_edition: Dis Manibus Flavia Herenia sibi se viva fecit et Flaviae Maximillae filiae quae vixit annos XL
EDCS-11301577.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavia Herenia made this for herself while she was alive, and for Flavia Maximilla, her daughter, who lived for 40 years.
EDCS-11301577.summary: This is a funerary inscription prepared by Flavia Herenia for herself during her lifetime, and for her deceased daughter Flavia Maximilla, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301578.text_edition: Dis Manibus Flavia Marcia vixit nnos LXX ex quibus nnos XXV mecum dulciter vixit Caius Valerius Aquilinus filius Valeri Viatoris coniugi dulcissimae bene merenti simul et sibi vivo posuit
EDCS-11301578.translation: To the Divine Manes. Flavia Marcia lived 70 years, of which she lived 25 years sweetly with me. Gaius Valerius Aquilinus, son of Valerius Viator, placed this for his sweetest, well-deserving wife, and for himself while living.
EDCS-11301578.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Marcia, erected by her husband Gaius Valerius Aquilinus, who also set it up for himself during his lifetime.

EDCS-11301579.text_edition: Dis Manibus Flavia Prisca vixit annos XXVII hic sita est Lucius Aemilius Pulcher
EDCS-11301579.translation: To the Divine Manes. Flavia Prisca lived 27 years. She is buried here. Lucius Aemilius Pulcher.
EDCS-11301579.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Prisca, likely erected by Lucius Aemilius Pulcher.

EDCS-11301580.text_edition: PI T vixit annos Flavia Clodia coniugi bene merenti posuit
EDCS-11301580.translation: P.I.T. lived for [---] years. Flavia Clodia placed this for her well-deserving husband.
EDCS-11301580.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Flavia Clodia for her deserving husband, whose name is missing, but who lived for an unstated number of years.

EDCS-11301581.text_edition: Flavius Hermes Flavio Dom et sibi posuit
EDCS-11301581.translation: Flavius Hermes placed this for Flavius Domitius and for himself.
EDCS-11301581.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Flavius Hermes for Flavius Domitius and also for himself, likely a tomb or a shared monument.

EDCS-11301583.text_edition: vixit annos  Heraclius vixit nnos V et Exsuperatus vixit annos  III  Septimius Herculanus pater et Aurelia Dinentilla mater filiis dulcissimis et sibi se viventibus posuerunt
EDCS-11301583.translation: Heraclius lived 5 years, and Exsuperatus lived 3 years. Septimius Herculanus, father, and Aurelia Dinentilla, mother, placed this for their sweetest sons and for themselves while living.
EDCS-11301583.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Septimius Herculanus and Aurelia Dinentilla to their deceased sons, Heraclius and Exsuperatus, and for themselves in advance of their own deaths.

EDCS-11301584.text_edition: Dis Manibus Iulia Antonia vixit annos XXIX et Calbo alumnus vixit annos V Achilleus vilicus coniugi et alumno bene merentibus et Antoni Invictianus Achillius Achillia matri dulcissimae bene merenti posuerunt
EDCS-11301584.translation: To the Divine Manes. Iulia Antonia lived 29 years, and Calbus, her foster son, lived 5 years. Achilleus, the estate manager, placed this for his well-deserving wife and foster son, and Antoni Invictianus Achillius and Achillia placed this for their sweetest, well-deserving mother.
EDCS-11301584.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Achilleus, a vilicus, to his wife Iulia Antonia and foster son Calbus, and additionally by Antoni Invictianus Achillius and Achillia to their mother Iulia Antonia.

EDCS-11301585.text_edition: vixit annos Marcia quAESPTAE marito et filio bene merentibus posuit et Aureliae Vitaliae filiae et sibi se vivis posuit
EDCS-11301585.translation: Marcia [---] lived years. She placed this for her well-deserving husband and son, and for her daughter Aurelia Vitalia and for herself while living.
EDCS-11301585.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Marcia for her husband, son, and daughter Aurelia Vitalia, and for herself during her lifetime.

EDCS-11301586.text_edition: Dis Manibus Salvia Ingenua vixit nnos VII hic sita est Salvius Perpetuus et Valeria Musa filiae bene merenti fecerunt
EDCS-11301586.translation: To the Divine Manes. Salvia Ingenua lived 7 years. She is buried here. Salvius Perpetuus and Valeria Musa made this for their well-deserving daughter.
EDCS-11301586.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Salvia Ingenua, erected by her parents Salvius Perpetuus and Valeria Musa.

EDCS-11301587.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Lucius Septimius Dus.
EDCS-11301587.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Septimius Dus, dedicated to the Manes.

EDCS-11301589.text_edition: Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius Secundus vixit nnos LV Septimia Gallitta coniugi bene merenti VA
EDCS-11301589.translation: To the Divine Manes. Marcus Ulpius Secundus lived 55 years. Septimia Gallitta [placed this] for her well-deserving husband. [---]
EDCS-11301589.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Ulpius Secundus, erected by his wife Septimia Gallitta.

EDCS-11301590.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Valens AR.
EDCS-11301590.summary: This short inscription is a funerary dedication to Valens AR, likely made to the Divine Spirits.

EDCS-11301591.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sextus Valerius.
EDCS-11301591.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication to Sextus Valerius, offered to the Manes.

EDCS-11301592.text_edition: LLG Marcus Valentinus vixit annos XX fili eius qui et here patri optimo bene merenti faciendum curaverunt
EDCS-11301592.translation: May the ground be light for him. Marcus Valentinus lived 20 years. His sons, who are also his heirs, had this made for their excellent, well-deserving father.
EDCS-11301592.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Valentinus, set up by his sons and heirs.

EDCS-11301593.text_edition: Dis Manibus Valerius Valerianus vixit annos LX Valerius Aquilinus parenti pientissimo una cum Claudio Caecilio ex delegato bene merenti posuerunt
EDCS-11301593.translation: To the Divine Manes. Valerius Valerianus lived 60 years. Valerius Aquilinus, along with Claudius Caecilius, by delegation, placed this for their most devoted, well-deserving parent.
EDCS-11301593.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Valerius Aquilinus and Claudius Caecilius for their deceased parent, Valerius Valerianus, acting on a delegated instruction.

EDCS-11301596.text_edition: Templum effectum per Cassium Achilleum et Publium Valerium Crescentem opifices inst
EDCS-11301596.translation: The temple was built by Cassius Achilleus and Publius Valerius Crescens, artisans. [---]
EDCS-11301596.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple by the artisans Cassius Achilleus and Publius Valerius Crescens.

EDCS-11301599.text_edition: inus miles legionis X  invicto ex voto
EDCS-11301599.translation: [---]inus, soldier of the Tenth Legion, the Invincible, according to a vow.
EDCS-11301599.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by a soldier of the Tenth Legion, likely for a deity, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-11301600.text_edition: Dis Manibus securitati perpetuae Aureli ni dignissimi viri qui vixit annos LV perfecit in mil(itia) annos XX fili Licini karissimi vixit annos XXX Zelia Iustina coniux ex inpendio eorum donavit bene merentibus
EDCS-11301600.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead, for the eternal security of Aurelius, a most worthy man, who lived 55 years, served 20 years in the military, and of Licinius, his dearest son, who lived 30 years. Zelia Justina, his wife, gave this at their expense to those who deserved well.
EDCS-11301600.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the dedication of a tomb by Zelia Justina to her husband Aurelius and her son Licinius, commemorating their lives and military service, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-11301601.text_edition: Hic situs est nomine Gaudentius vultu ac positione decorus qui annis vitam egit amplius viginta aeternam sedem PIVS LAV
EDCS-11301601.translation: Here lies Gaudentius, handsome in appearance and posture, who lived for more than twenty years, his eternal resting place. Pius Lav...
EDCS-11301601.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaudentius, describing his physical attributes and age at death, with the end of the inscription being incomplete.

EDCS-11301609.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Iovi Maximo  Cohortali Paterno Aurelius Catandio posuit aram libiens sic ex boto sic feliciter
EDCS-11301609.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Cohortalis, Paternus, Aurelius Catandio willingly set up this altar, in accordance with his vow, happily.
EDCS-11301609.summary: This is a votive altar dedicated by Aurelius Catandio to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Cohortalis Paternus, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11301610.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo  Cohortali Aurelius Iusti=ITnianus#Iustinianus#IUSITNIANUS pro se et suos sic votum libens posuit
EDCS-11301610.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Cohortalis, Aurelius Justinianus for himself and his kin, willingly set up this vow.
EDCS-11301610.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Aurelius Justinianus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Cohortalis on behalf of himself and his family, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-11301611.text_edition: Aurelius Batao pro se et suos sic votu sic posuit
EDCS-11301611.translation: Aurelius Batao for himself and his people thus placed this vow.
EDCS-11301611.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Aurelius Batao for himself and his family, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-11301612.text_edition: Aurel   pro salute sua et suorum votum libens posuit
EDCS-11301612.translation: Aurel[ius ---] for his own health and that of his family, willingly placed this vow.
EDCS-11301612.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by an individual named Aurelius for his and his family's health, willingly fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-12100013.text_edition: Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis tribus et cohortibus decem et sex quae appellantur I civium Romanorum et II Flavia Commagenorum sagittaria et II nnum veterana et I Brittonum |(miliaria) Ulpia torquata civium Romanorum et et I Britannica |(miliaria) civium Romanorum et I Ituraeorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et I Augusta Ituraeorum et I Vindelicorum civium Romanorum pia fidelis et I nnum veterana et I Montanorum et II Gallorum Pannonica et II Hispanorum et II nnum |(miliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et II Gallorum Macedonica et III Campestris civium Romanorum et IIII Cypria civium Romanorum et V Gallorum et VIII Raetorum et sunt in Dacia sub Decimo Terentio Scauriano quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione a Iulio Sabino quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas pridie Idus Octobres Caio Iulio Proculo Caio Aburnio Valente consulibus cohortis I Montanorum cui praeest Cornelius Felicior ex pedite Marco Herennio Marci filio Polymitae Berensi et Ianuario filio eius et Marcello filio eius et Lucanae filiae eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam  Imperator Caesar divi Nervae filius Nerva Traianus Augustus Germanicus Dacicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XIII imperator VI consul V pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis tribus et cohortibus decem et sex quae appellantur I civium Romanorum et I Flavia Commagenorum sagittaria et II nnum veterana et I Brittonum |(miliaria) Ulpia torquata civium Romanorum et I Britannica |(miliaria) civium Romanorum et I Ituraeorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et I Augusta Ituraeorum et I Vindelicorum civium Romanorum pia fidelis et I nnum veterana et I Montanorum et II Gallorum Pannonica et II Hispanorum et II nnum |(miliaria) civium Romanorum pia fidelis et II Gallorum Macedonica et III Campestris civium Romanorum et IIII Cypria civium Romanorum et V Gallorum et VIII Raetorum et sunt in Dacia sub Decimo Terentio Scauriano quinis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione a Iulio Sabino quorum  nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas pridie Idus Octobres Caio Iulio Proculo Caio Aburnio Valente consulibus cohortis I Montanorum cui praeest Cornelius Felicior ex pedite Marco Herennio Marci filio Polymitae Berensi et Ianuario et Marcello filiis eius et Lucanae filiae eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae  Tiberi Iuli Urbani Publi Corneli Alexandri Luci Pulli Verecundi Publi Atini Amerimni Cai Iuli Parati Cai Tuticani Saturnini Marci Iuli Clementis
EDCS-12100013.translation: The Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, acclaimed emperor for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, to the cavalrymen and infantrymen who served in three alae and sixteen cohorts, which are called the I Civium Romanorum, II Flavia Commagenorum Sagittaria, II Vetera, I Brittonum (milliaria) Ulpia Torquata Civium Romanorum, I Britannica (milliaria) Civium Romanorum, I Ituraeorum, I Thracum Civium Romanorum, I Augusta Ituraeorum, I Vindelicorum Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, I Vetera, I Montanorum, II Gallorum Pannonica, II Hispanorum, II (milliaria) Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, II Gallorum Macedonica, III Campestris Civium Romanorum, IIII Cypria Civium Romanorum, V Gallorum, and VIII Raetorum, and are in Dacia under Decimus Terentius Scaurianus, having completed twenty-five or more stipends, honorably discharged by Julius Sabinus, whose names are written below, granted citizenship to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted, or, if they were bachelors, with those they might marry thereafter, provided each married only one wife. On the day before the Ides of October, with Gaius Julius Proculus and Gaius Aburnius Valens as consuls, for the I Cohort of Montani, commanded by Cornelius Felicior, to the infantryman Marcus Herennius, son of Marcus, a Polymita of Berensis, and to his son Januarius, and to his son Marcellus, and to his daughter Lucana. Transcribed and checked from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the Divine Augustus, near the temple of Minerva. The Emperor Caesar Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, son of divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, acclaimed emperor for the 6th time, consul for the 5th time, father of the country, to the cavalrymen and infantrymen who served in three alae and sixteen cohorts, which are called the I Civium Romanorum, I Flavia Commagenorum Sagittaria, II Vetera, I Brittonum (milliaria) Ulpia Torquata Civium Romanorum, I Britannica (milliaria) Civium Romanorum, I Ituraeorum, I Thracum Civium Romanorum, I Augusta Ituraeorum, I Vindelicorum Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, I Vetera, I Montanorum, II Gallorum Pannonica, II Hispanorum, II (milliaria) Civium Romanorum Pia Fidelis, II Gallorum Macedonica, III Campestris Civium Romanorum, IIII Cypria Civium Romanorum, V Gallorum, and VIII Raetorum, and are in Dacia under Decimus Terentius Scaurianus, having completed twenty-five or more stipends, honorably discharged by Julius Sabinus, whose names are written below, granted citizenship to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted, or, if they were bachelors, with those they might marry thereafter, provided each married only one wife. On the day before the Ides of October, with Gaius Julius Proculus and Gaius Aburnius Valens as consuls, for the I Cohort of Montani, commanded by Cornelius Felicior, to the infantryman Marcus Herennius, son of Marcus, a Polymita of Berensis, and to his sons Januarius and Marcellus, and to his daughter Lucana. Transcribed and checked from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome. Witnesses: Tiberius Julius Urbanus, Publius Cornelius Alexander, Lucius Pullius Verecundus, Publius Atinius Amerimnus, Gaius Julius Paratus, Gaius Tuticanus Saturninus, Marcus Julius Clemens.
EDCS-12100013.summary: This is a Roman military diploma issued by Emperor Trajan on October 14, 109 AD, granting Roman citizenship and conubium (right of legal marriage) to discharged auxiliary soldiers, specifically an infantryman Marcus Herennius and his family from the First Cohort of Montani, who served in Dacia.

EDCS-12100780.text_edition: Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VI imperator XIIII pater patriae censor consul VI designatus VII peditibus et equitibus qui militant in cohortibus decem quae appellantur I Antiochensium et I Sugambrorum veterana et I Raetorum et I Lusitanorum et III et IV et V et VII et VIII Gallorum et Cisipadensium et sunt in Moesia sub Sexto Vettuleno Ceriale qui quina et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerant quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem IIII Kalendas Maias Caesare Augusti filio Domitiano III Lucio Pasidieno Firmo consulibus cohortis I Raetorum cui praeest Caius Quintius Laberius Tutor Sabinianus pediti Herae Serapionis filio Antiochia descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio post piscinam in tribunali deorum  Imperator Caesar Vespasianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VI imperator XIIII pater patriae censor consul VI designatus VII peditibus et equitibus qui militant in cohortibus decem quae appellantur I Antiochensium et I Sugambrorum veterana et I Raetorum et I Lusitanorum et III et IV et V et VII et VIII Gallorum et Cisipadensium et sunt in Moesia sub Sexto Vettuleno Ceriale qui quina et vicena stipendia aut plura meruerant quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si qui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli  singulas ante diem IIII Kalendas Maias Caesare Augusti filio Domitiano III Lucio Pasidieno Firmo consulibus cohortis I Raetoriae sic cui praeest Caius Quintius Laberius Tutor Sabinianus pediti Herae Serapionis filio Antiochia descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in Capitolio  Luci Domiti Luci fili Collina Veri Publi Coeli Quinti fili Falerna Bruti Rufi Quinti Iuli Lentuli Quinti Aquili Cai fili Voltinia Campani Titi Lossi Titi fili Galeria Severi Sexti Lossi Titi fili Galeria Apollinaris Publi Atini Publi fili Velina Augustalis
EDCS-12100780.translation: The Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, acclaimed emperor for the 14th time, father of the country, censor, consul for the 6th time, designated consul for the 7th time, to the infantrymen and cavalrymen who are serving in the ten cohorts called I Antiochensium, I Sugambrorum Veterana, I Raetorum, I Lusitanorum, and the III, IV, V, VII, VIII Gallorum, and Cisipadensium, and are in Moesia under Sextus Vettulenus Cerialis, who have earned twenty-five or more stipends, whose names are written below, granted citizenship to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted, or, if they were bachelors, with those they might marry thereafter, provided each married only one wife. On the 4th day before the Kalends of May, with Caesar Domitian, son of Augustus, for the 3rd time, and Lucius Pasidienus Firmus as consuls, for the I Cohort of Raeti, commanded by Gaius Quintius Laberius Tutor Sabinianus, to the infantryman Hera, son of Serapion, from Antioch. Transcribed and checked from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the Capitol, behind the piscina, at the tribunal of the gods. The Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, acclaimed emperor for the 14th time, father of the country, censor, consul for the 6th time, designated consul for the 7th time, to the infantrymen and cavalrymen who are serving in the ten cohorts called I Antiochensium, I Sugambrorum Veterana, I Raetorum, I Lusitanorum, and the III, IV, V, VII, VIII Gallorum, and Cisipadensium, and are in Moesia under Sextus Vettulenus Cerialis, who have earned twenty-five or more stipends, whose names are written below, granted citizenship to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted, or, if they were bachelors, with those they might marry thereafter, provided each married only one wife. On the 4th day before the Kalends of May, with Caesar Domitian, son of Augustus, for the 3rd time, and Lucius Pasidienus Firmus as consuls, for the I Cohort of Raeti, commanded by Gaius Quintius Laberius Tutor Sabinianus, to the infantryman Hera, son of Serapion, from Antioch. Transcribed and checked from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the Capitol. Witnesses: Lucius Domitius Verus, son of Lucius, of the Collina tribe; Publius Coelius Brutus Rufus, son of Quintus, of the Falerna tribe; Quintus Julius Lentulus; Quintus Aquilius Campanus, son of Gaius, of the Voltinia tribe; Titus Lossius Severus, son of Titus, of the Galeria tribe; Sextus Lossius Apollinaris, son of Titus, of the Galeria tribe; Publius Atinius Augustalis, son of Publius, of the Velina tribe.
EDCS-12100780.summary: This is a Roman military diploma issued by Emperor Vespasian on April 28, 74 AD, granting Roman citizenship and conubium to auxiliary soldiers, specifically an infantryman Hera, and his family from the First Cohort of Raeti, who served in Moesia.

EDCS-12100784.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12100784.text_edition: Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XV imperator XXII consul XVII censor perpetuus pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militant in ala praetoria et cohortibus decem I Lusitanorum et I Cretum et I Montanorum et I Cilicum et I Flavia Hispanorum milliaria et II Flavia Commagenorum et IIII Raetorum et V Hispanorum et VI Thracum et VII Breucorum civium Romanorum quae sunt in Moesia superiore sub Cnaeo Aemilio Cicatricula Pompeio Longino qui quina et vicena plurave stipendia meruerunt item dimissis honesta missione emeritis stipendiis quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem IIII Idus Iulias Tito Prifernio Paeto Quinto Fabio Postumino consulibus cohortis VI Thracum cui praeest Claudius Alpinus pediti Dolenti Sublusi filio Besso et Valenti filio eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam  Quinti Aemili Soterichi Cai Iuli Vitalis Quinti Pompei Homeri Publi Atini Amerimni Publi Corneli Alexandri Luci Pulli Trophimi Quinti Carrinati Quadrati  Imperator Caesar divi Vespasiani filius Domitianus Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XV imperator XXII consul XVII censor perpetuus pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militant in ala praetoria et cohortibus decem I Lusitanorum et I Cretum et I Montanorum et I Cilicum et I Flavia Hispanorum milliaria et II Flavia Commagenorum et IIII Raetorum et V Hispanorum et VI Thracum et VII Breucorum civium Romanorum et quae sunt in Moesia superiore sub Cnaeo Aemilio Cicatricula Pompeio Longino qui quina et vicena plurave stipendia meruerunt item dimissis honesta missione emeritis stipendiis quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem IIII Idus Iulias Tito Prifernio Paeto Quinto Fabio Postumino consulibus cohortis VI Thracum cui praeest Claudius Alpinus pediti Dolenti Sublusi filio Besso et Valenti filio eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae
EDCS-12100784.translation: The Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus Germanicus, son of divine Vespasian, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 15th time, acclaimed emperor for the 22nd time, consul for the 17th time, perpetual censor, father of the country, to the cavalrymen and infantrymen who are serving in the praetorian ala and in the ten cohorts: I Lusitanorum, I Cretum, I Montanorum, I Cilicum, I Flavia Hispanorum Milliaria, II Flavia Commagenorum, IIII Raetorum, V Hispanorum, VI Thracum, and VII Breucorum Civium Romanorum, which are in Upper Moesia under Gnaeus Aemilius Cicatricula Pompeius Longinus, who have earned twenty-five or more stipends, and likewise to those discharged with honorable mission after completing their stipends, whose names are written below, granted citizenship to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted, or, if they were bachelors, with those they might marry thereafter, provided each married only one wife. On the 4th day before the Ides of July, with Titus Prifernius Paetus and Quintus Fabius Postuminus as consuls, for the VI Cohort of Thracians, commanded by Claudius Alpinus, to the infantryman Dolens, son of Sublusus, a Besso, and to his son Valens. Transcribed and checked from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the Divine Augustus, near the temple of Minerva. Witnesses: Quintus Aemilius Soterichus, Gaius Julius Vitalis, Quintus Pompeius Homerus, Publius Atinius Amerimnus, Publius Cornelius Alexander, Lucius Pullius Trophimus, Quintus Carrinatius Quadratus. The Emperor Caesar Domitian Augustus Germanicus, son of divine Vespasian, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 15th time, acclaimed emperor for the 22nd time, consul for the 17th time, perpetual censor, father of the country, to the cavalrymen and infantrymen who are serving in the praetorian ala and in the ten cohorts: I Lusitanorum, I Cretum, I Montanorum, I Cilicum, I Flavia Hispanorum Milliaria, II Flavia Commagenorum, IIII Raetorum, V Hispanorum, VI Thracum, and VII Breucorum Civium Romanorum, which are in Upper Moesia under Gnaeus Aemilius Cicatricula Pompeius Longinus, who have earned twenty-five or more stipends, and likewise to those discharged with honorable mission after completing their stipends, whose names are written below, granted citizenship to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted, or, if they were bachelors, with those they might marry thereafter, provided each married only one wife. On the 4th day before the Ides of July, with Titus Prifernius Paetus and Quintus Fabius Postuminus as consuls, for the VI Cohort of Thracians, commanded by Claudius Alpinus, to the infantryman Dolens, son of Sublusus, a Besso, and to his son Valens. Transcribed and checked from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome.
EDCS-12100784.summary: This is a Roman military diploma issued by Emperor Domitian on July 12, 91 AD, granting Roman citizenship and conubium to discharged auxiliary soldiers, specifically an infantryman Dolens and his son Valens from the Sixth Cohort of Thracians, who served in Upper Moesia.

EDCS-12200269.text_edition: Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate IIIII consuli II patri patriae et Sabiniae Tranquillinae Augustae NE Lucio Catio Celere legato Augusti pro praetore
EDCS-12200269.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the country, and to Sabinia Tranquillina Augusta, by Lucius Catius Celer, legate of Augustus pro praetore.
EDCS-12200269.summary: This is a monumental dedication, likely a base for a statue, honoring Emperor Gordian III and his wife Sabinia Tranquillina, erected by Lucius Catius Celer, a provincial governor, likely dating to 240 AD.

EDCS-12200270.text_edition: Manibus Dis Mucala Dydiae vixit nns sexaginta Pirusala Pirula sic fecit coniug=LOEi#coniugi#COLOEI suo
EDCS-12200270.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Mucala Dydias lived sixty years. Pirusala Pirula made this for her husband.
EDCS-12200270.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely a tombstone, dedicated by Pirusala Pirula to her husband Mucala Dydias, commemorating his age at death, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-12300271.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300271.text_edition: Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IIII consul III peditibus qui militaverunt in cohorte I Flavia Bessorum quae est in Macedonia sub Octavio Antonino quinque et viginti stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit et conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut siqui caelibes essent cum iis quas postea duxissent dumtaxat singuli singulas ante diem III Kalendas Iulias Caio Publicio Marcello Lucio Rutilio Propinquo consulibus cohortis I Flaviae Bessorum cui praeest Aulus Aelius Sollemnianus ex pedite Marco Antonio Timi filio Timi Hierapoli et Doroturmae Dotochae filiae uxori eius Tricornio et Secundo filio eius et Marcellinae filiae eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aenea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam  Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate IIII consul III peditibus qui militaverunt in cohorte I Flavia Bessorum quae est in Macedonia sub Octavio Antonino quinque et viginti stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt ipsis liberis posterisque eorum civitatem dedit conubium cum uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum est civitas iis data aut si
EDCS-12300271.translation: The Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, to the infantrymen who served in the I Flavia Bessorum cohort, which is in Macedonia under Octavius Antoninus, having completed twenty-five stipends, honorably discharged, whose names are written below, granted citizenship to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted, or, if they were bachelors, with those they might marry thereafter, provided each married only one wife. On the 3rd day before the Kalends of July, with Gaius Publicius Marcellus and Lucius Rutilius Propinquus as consuls, for the I Flavia Bessorum cohort, commanded by Aulus Aelius Sollemnianus, to the infantryman Marcus Antonius, son of Timus, a Timus from Hierapolis, and to Doroturma, daughter of Dotocha, his wife, and to their son Triconius, and to their son Secundus, and to their daughter Marcellina. Transcribed and checked from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome on the wall behind the temple of the Divine Augustus, near the temple of Minerva. The Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of divine Nerva, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, to the infantrymen who served in the I Flavia Bessorum cohort, which is in Macedonia under Octavius Antoninus, having completed twenty-five stipends, honorably discharged, whose names are written below, granted citizenship to them, their children, and their descendants, and the right of marriage with the wives they had at the time citizenship was granted, or, if they were bachelors, with those they might marry thereafter, provided each married only one wife. On the 3rd day before the Kalends of July, with Gaius Publicius Marcellus and Lucius Rutilius Propinquus as consuls, for the I Flavia Bessorum cohort, commanded by Aulus Aelius Sollemnianus, to the infantryman Marcus Antonius, son of Timus, a Timus from Hierapolis, and to Doroturma, daughter of Dotocha, his wife, and to their son Triconius, and to their son Secundus, and to their daughter Marcellina. Transcribed and checked from the bronze tablet which is affixed in Rome.
EDCS-12300271.summary: This is a Roman military diploma issued by Emperor Hadrian on June 29, 120 AD, granting Roman citizenship and conubium to discharged auxiliary soldiers, specifically an infantryman Marcus Antonius and his family from the First Flavian Cohort of Bessi, who served in Macedonia.

EDCS-12700087.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Depulsori pro salute Titi Iuli Optati circitoris portorii publici Lucius Licinius Terminalis votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-12700087.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Depulsor, for the well-being of Titus Iulius Optatus, a circuit officer of the public customs, Lucius Licinius Terminalis willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-12700087.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Depulsor by Lucius Licinius Terminalis for the health of a customs officer.

EDCS-12800392.text_edition: Publius Aelius Calvinus Marcus Aurelius Severinus bbenefficiarii consulares legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis votum solverunt libentes merito  Praesente et Extricato consulibus
EDCS-12800392.translation: Publius Aelius Calvinus and Marcus Aurelius Severinus, consular beneficiarii of the Legio II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis, willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow. In the consulship of Praesens and Extricatus.
EDCS-12800392.summary: This votive inscription records two consular beneficiarii of the Second Legion Adiutrix fulfilling a vow, dated by the names of the consuls Praesens and Extricatus.

EDCS-13900438.text_edition: Dardaniae sacrum Philoxenus Augggustorum nnnostrorum servus vilicus stationis Aquarum Bassianarum templum ex voto a solo restituit votum libens solvit Augustas Senecione et Aemiliano consulibus
EDCS-13900438.translation: Sacred to Dardania. Philoxenus, a slave of our Augusti and manager of the station of Aquae Bassianae, rebuilt the temple from its foundations according to a vow, and willingly fulfilled his vow, in the consulship of Augustus, Senecio, and Aemilianus.
EDCS-13900438.summary: This inscription records Philoxenus, an imperial slave and manager, rebuilding a temple to Dardania from its foundations as a vow, with the date likely referring to emperors and consuls.

EDCS-13900472.translation: Caise placed this according to a vow.
EDCS-13900472.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Caise, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-15800384.text_edition: Dis Manibus Iuliani Daphni Thyatireni vixit annos XXX hic situs est Crispinus Daphni buleuta Thyatirenus fratri faciendum curavit
EDCS-15800384.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulianus Daphnus from Thyatira lived for 30 years; he lies here. Crispinus Daphnus, a councilman of Thyatira, arranged for this to be made for his brother.
EDCS-15800384.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulianus Daphnus, erected by his brother Crispinus Daphnus, a councilman from Thyatira.

EDCS-15900080.text_edition: Dianae Reginae Iulius Romanio et Aurelius Constans pro salute sua votum posuerunt
EDCS-15900080.translation: To Diana Regina, Iulius Romanio and Aurelius Constans placed a vow for their own well-being.
EDCS-15900080.summary: This is a votive inscription to Diana Regina, dedicated by Iulius Romanio and Aurelius Constans for their own health.

EDCS-15900082.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo ex boto sic Caca et Iulius fratres
EDCS-15900082.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Caca and Julius, brothers, thus [made this] according to a vow.
EDCS-15900082.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by two brothers, Caca and Julius, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-15900083.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Roman deities Liber Pater and Libera.

EDCS-15900084.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Atadis Dorani filius qui militavit numero DDalmatarum centuria Calvini vixit annos -XX Romus qui militavit in numerum sic Dalmatarum centuria vi
EDCS-15900084.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Atadis, son of Doranus, who served in the numerus of Dalmatians, in the centuria of Calvinus, lived for XX years. Romus, who served in the numerus of Dalmatians, in the sixth centuria.
EDCS-15900084.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Atadis, a soldier from a Dalmatian unit, and mentions Romus, another soldier from a Dalmatian unit, likely indicating a joint monument or a continuation of the same text.

EDCS-15900085.text_edition: Dis  Manibus  Aurelio Mundo exarcho numeri Dalmatarum vixit annos XXX et Aureliae Surae vixit annos XX et Aureliae Augustae vixit annos VIIII Aurelius Myrcianus sic parentibus bene merentibus posuit
EDCS-15900085.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Aurelius Mundus, exarch of the numerus of Dalmatians, who lived 30 years, and for Aurelia Sura, who lived 20 years, and for Aurelia Augusta, who lived 9 years. Aurelius Myrcianus placed this for his well-deserving parents.
EDCS-15900085.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Aurelius Myrcianus to his parents, Aurelius Mundus (an exarch of a Dalmatian unit) and Aurelia Sura, and to Aurelia Augusta.

EDCS-15900086.text_edition: Dis  Manibus  Aureli Maximiniani circitoris vixit nnos LII Aurelius Maximus veteranus
EDCS-15900086.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Aurelius Maximinianus, a circuit officer, who lived 52 years. Aurelius Maximus, a veteran.
EDCS-15900086.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Aurelius Maximinianus, a circuit officer, and was erected by Aurelius Maximus, a veteran, likely a relative or colleague.

EDCS-15900090.text_edition: Dis Manibus Lucio Valerio Basso veterano legionis VII Claudiae vixit annos LX Lucius Valerius Valens
EDCS-15900090.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Valerius Bassus, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia, who lived 60 years. Lucius Valerius Valens.
EDCS-15900090.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Lucius Valerius Bassus, a veteran of the Seventh Legion Claudia, by Lucius Valerius Valens, presumably a family member.

EDCS-15900091.text_edition: Dis Manibus Caio Valerio Cai filio Papiria Firmo veterano legionis VII Claudiae piae fidelis conscripto decurioni Ratiariae vixit nnos LXXV Caius Valerius Firmus decurioni coloniae eiusdem patri pientissimo faciendum curavit
EDCS-15900091.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Gaius Valerius Firmus, son of Gaius, of the Papiria tribe, a veteran of the Legio VII Claudia Pia Fidelis, a conscripted decurion of Ratiaria, who lived 75 years. Gaius Valerius Firmus, decurion of the same colony, arranged for this to be made for his most devoted father.
EDCS-15900091.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Gaius Valerius Firmus (a decurion) for his father, also named Gaius Valerius Firmus, a veteran of the Seventh Legion Claudia and a decurion of Ratiaria.

EDCS-15900093.text_edition: et Aureli Longini vixit annos VI et Aureli Bessi vixit annos V Aurelia Matiae sic et Aurelius Ursus ducenarius bene merentibus posuerunt
EDCS-15900093.translation: And to Aurelius Longinus, who lived for 6 years, and to Aurelius Bessus, who lived for 5 years. Aurelia Matia likewise and Aurelius Ursus, a ducenarius, placed this for the well-deserving.
EDCS-15900093.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording two young boys, Aurelius Longinus and Aurelius Bessus, set up by Aurelia Matia and Aurelius Ursus, a ducenarius, likely their parents or guardians, probably in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-15900094.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Cai Memmi Proculi Iunioris vixit annos II menses IX dies X Caius Memmius Proculus decurio coloniae Ratiariae et Cornelia Eventa filio piissimo
EDCS-15900094.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Gaius Memmius Proculus Junior, who lived for 2 years, 9 months, and 10 days. Gaius Memmius Proculus, decurion of the colony of Ratiaria, and Cornelia Eventa [placed this] for their most dutiful son.
EDCS-15900094.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy, Gaius Memmius Proculus Junior, erected by his parents, Gaius Memmius Proculus, a decurion of Ratiaria, and Cornelia Eventa, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16100277.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Aurelius Bassus sacerdos servus eius
EDCS-16100277.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Aurelius Bassus, priest, and his slave.
EDCS-16100277.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by Aurelius Bassus, a priest, and his slave.

EDCS-16200805.text_edition: Dis Manibus Lucio Flaminio Iuliano decurioni coloniae Ratiariae vixit [annos &
EDCS-16200805.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Lucius Flaminius Iulianus, decurion of the colony of Ratiaria. He lived for [---] years.
EDCS-16200805.summary: This is a funerary inscription (tombstone) dedicated to Lucius Flaminius Iulianus, a decurion of the Roman colony of Ratiaria, likely erected by his family or associates in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16201641.text_edition: Dis Manibus  Luci Tetti Rufi decurionis pontificis coloniae Ratiariae Fonteianus frater
EDCS-16201641.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Lucius Tettius Rufus, decurion and pontifex of the colony of Ratiaria. Fonteianus, his brother [placed this].
EDCS-16201641.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Tettius Rufus, a decurion and pontifex in the colony of Ratiaria, set up by his brother Fonteianus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16300160.text_edition: Pali Sancto Pastorali Caius Iulius Valens decurio coloniae Ratiariae
EDCS-16300160.translation: To Holy Palis Pastoralis. Gaius Iulius Valens, decurion of the colony of Ratiaria.
EDCS-16300160.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Gaius Iulius Valens, a decurion of the colony of Ratiaria, to the local deity Palis Pastoralis, indicating a religious offering or vow fulfillment.

EDCS-16300576.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the goddess Diana, associated with the Roman province of Dacia Ripensis, suggesting a religious context from that region.

EDCS-16400137.text_edition: Dis Manibus Gaio Valerio Alexandro qui vixit annos L Iulia Pollitta coniux et Valerii Marcianus ornatus ornamentis decurionalibus Marcus Philiscus Alexandria Iulia filii patri bene merenti
EDCS-16400137.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. For Gaius Valerius Alexander, who lived for 50 years. Julia Pollitta, his wife, and Valerii Marcianus, honored with decurional insignia, and Marcus Philiscus Alexandria Julia, his sons, [placed this] for their well-deserving father.
EDCS-16400137.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Valerius Alexander, who lived to 50 years, dedicated by his wife Julia Pollitta and his sons, Valerius Marcianus and Marcus Philiscus Alexandria Julia, probably in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16400354.text_edition: Sub cura Boniti praepositi legionis VII
EDCS-16400354.translation: Under the care of Bonitus, commander of the Seventh Legion.
EDCS-16400354.summary: This inscription records an activity or construction project undertaken under the supervision of Bonitus, the commander (praepositus) of the Seventh Legion, likely dating to the Late Roman Empire.

EDCS-16400355.translation: Marg[---], commander of the cavalry, Mar[---].
EDCS-16400355.summary: This fragmentary inscription indicates an activity or structure related to a cavalry unit under the command of 'Marg[---]', with another name 'Mar[---]' possibly also involved, likely from the Late Roman period due to the title 'praepositus'.

EDCS-16600413.text_edition: Legio IIII sub cura Hermae principis
EDCS-16600413.translation: The Fourth Legion, under the care of Herma, princeps.
EDCS-16600413.summary: This inscription denotes an action or structure overseen by Herma, a 'princeps' (a senior non-commissioned officer), associated with the Fourth Legion, characteristic of Late Roman military administration.

EDCS-16600414.text_edition: Legio VII Claudia sub cura Victorini praepositi EB
EDCS-16600414.translation: The Seventh Claudia Legion, under the care of Victorinus, commander. EB
EDCS-16600414.summary: This inscription refers to an undertaking by the Seventh Claudia Legion supervised by its commander, Victorinus, likely during the Late Roman Empire, with 'EB' being an abbreviation or part of the text.

EDCS-16600415.text_edition: Legio VII Claudia sub cura Victorini praepositi Flavius Vitalianus
EDCS-16600415.translation: The Seventh Claudia Legion, under the care of Victorinus, commander. Flavius Vitalianus.
EDCS-16600415.summary: This inscription records a project or work by the Seventh Claudia Legion, under the command of Victorinus, also mentioning Flavius Vitalianus, likely as another individual involved in or responsible for the work, characteristic of the Late Roman period.

EDCS-16700323.text_edition: Legio IIII Flavia sub Dinitio praeposito
EDCS-16700323.translation: The Fourth Flavian Legion, under Dinitius, commander.
EDCS-16700323.summary: This inscription indicates an activity or construction by the Fourth Flavian Legion, under the leadership of its commander, Dinitius, reflecting Late Roman military organization.

EDCS-16700324.text_edition: Legio IIII Flavia sub cura Tatae praefecti
EDCS-16700324.translation: The Fourth Flavian Legion, under the care of Tata, prefect.
EDCS-16700324.summary: This inscription details a work or project by the Fourth Flavian Legion, supervised by Tata, who held the rank of prefect, typical of Late Roman military command structures.

EDCS-16700326.text_edition: Legio VII Claudia sub cura Victorini praepositi
EDCS-16700326.translation: The Seventh Claudian Legion, under the charge of Victorinus, praepositus.
EDCS-16700326.summary: This inscription records the building activity or presence of Legio VII Claudia under the command of a praepositus named Victorinus, likely dating to the late Roman period.

EDCS-16700327.text_edition: Legio VII sub cura Ursaci praefecti Argutio
EDCS-16700327.translation: The Seventh Legion, under the charge of Ursacius, prefect for Argutius.
EDCS-16700327.summary: This inscription indicates an activity or presence of Legio VII under the supervision of Ursacius, a prefect, possibly acting on behalf of Argutius, likely from the late Roman era.

EDCS-16700328.text_edition: Equites Dalmatae AR sub cura Veraci praepositi fecit Mucianus
EDCS-16700328.translation: Dalmation cavalry AR, under the charge of Verax, praepositus. Mucianus made this.
EDCS-16700328.summary: This inscription documents the construction or creation of something by Mucianus for the Dalmation cavalry (Equites Dalmatae), overseen by Verax, a praepositus, indicating a military context.

EDCS-16700329.text_edition: Equites sagittari sub cura Italici Br--
EDCS-16700329.translation: Mounted archers, under the charge of Italicus Br--.
EDCS-16700329.summary: This inscription refers to the mounted archers (Equites sagittarii) under the command of Italicus, whose full name is not preserved, likely indicating a military unit's activity or presence.

EDCS-16700331.translation: The three-horned standard of Valerius, praepositus.
EDCS-16700331.summary: This inscription identifies a 'tricornium' (possibly a standard or specific military device) belonging to Valerius, a praepositus, highlighting a military artifact from the late Roman period.

EDCS-16700332.text_edition: Castra Viminacensia sub cura Senecionis ducis
EDCS-16700332.translation: The camp of Viminacium, under the charge of Senecio, dux.
EDCS-16700332.summary: This inscription refers to the military camp at Viminacium, indicating it was under the command of Senecio, a dux, suggesting a late Roman military administrative context.

EDCS-16800215.text_edition: Iobi sic Optimo Maximo Dulicheno sic ex voto Aelii Silvanus het sic Leonides legC(ionis)#leg(ionis)#LEC signiferi bene mirenti sic
EDCS-16800215.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, in accordance with a vow. Aelius Silvanus and Leonides, standard-bearers of the legion, [placed this] for the well-deserving.
EDCS-16800215.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by two standard-bearers of a legion, Aelius Silvanus and Leonides, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16800238.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute Imperatoris domini nostri Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Sac Picta et Flavius Gugaque posuerunt
EDCS-16800238.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the health of our lord Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Sac Picta and Flavius Guga placed this.
EDCS-16800238.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by Sac Picta and Flavius Guga for the health of the emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus (Caracalla), dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-16800239.text_edition: Herculi Invicto Aurelius Herodes et Iulius Super pro salute collegii votum l(ibens) animo merenti posuerunt
